commit_message:
ncurses 6.2 - patch 20200531

+ correct configure version-check/warnng for g++ to allow for 10.x
+ re-enable "bel" in konsole-base (report by Nia Huang)
+ add linux-s entry (patch by Alexandre Montaron).
+ drop long-obsolete convert_configure.pl
+ add test/test_parm.c, for checking tparm changes.
+ improve parameter-checking for tparm, adding function _nc_tiparm() to
  handle the most-used case, which accepts only numeric parameters
  (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
+ use a more conservative estimate of the buffer-size in lib_tparm.c's
  save_text() and save_number(), in case the sprintf() function
  passes-through unexpected characters from a format specifier
  (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
+ add a check for end-of-string in cvtchar to handle a malformed
  string in infotocap (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in ncurses through v6.2-1. _nc_captoinfo in captoinfo.c has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when '^' is the last character in the string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the input string ends with '^' (i.e., the character after '^' is the null terminator), `*++sp` reads the null byte and `c` becomes 0. The function returns `len = 2`, causing the caller to advance the pointer 2 positions past the '^', skipping over the null terminator. This leads to the caller reading memory beyond the end of the string buffer in subsequent iterations, which is an out-of-bounds read. An attacker providing a crafted termcap string ending in '^' could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Octal escape parsing accepts non-octal digits (8, 9) and has no length limit
vulnerabilities.long_description: The octal escape sequence parser uses `isdigit()` instead of checking for octal digits (0-7). This means digits '8' and '9' are incorrectly processed as part of an octal value, producing incorrect character values due to the `8 * c` multiplication. Additionally, there is no limit on the number of digits consumed (standard C limits octal escapes to 3 digits), so an arbitrarily long sequence of digits will be consumed. While `c` is an `unsigned char` and wraps on overflow, the incorrect parsing can lead to unexpected character values being generated, potentially misinterpreting input in security-sensitive terminal capability processing.

commit_message:
Save stack space while handling errors

Because error handling (luaG_errormsg) uses slots from EXTRA_STACK,
and some errors can recur (e.g., string overflow while creating an
error message in 'luaG_runerror', or a C-stack overflow before calling
the message handler), the code should use stack slots with parsimony.

This commit fixes the bug "Lua-stack overflow when C stack overflows
while handling an error".
cve_desc: An issue in the component luaG_runerror of Lua v5.4.4 and below leads to a heap-buffer overflow when a recursive error occurs.

commit_message:
sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk

sctp_make_strreset_req() makes repeated calls to sctp_addto_chunk()
which will automatically account for padding on each call. inreq and
outreq are already 4 bytes aligned, but the payload is not and doing
SCTP_PAD4(a + b) (which _sctp_make_chunk() did implicitly here) is
different from SCTP_PAD4(a) + SCTP_PAD4(b) and not enough. It led to
possible attempt to use more buffer than it was allocated and triggered
a BUG_ON.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>
Cc: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Cc: Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in stream_len calculation due to __u16 multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable stream_len is declared as __u16 (unsigned 16-bit), and stream_num is also __u16. The multiplication `stream_num * sizeof(__u16)` can overflow the 16-bit range when stream_num is large (e.g., greater than 32767). This would cause stream_len to wrap around to a small value, leading to undersized buffer allocation via sctp_make_reconf, while the actual stream_list data passed to sctp_addto_chunk could be larger than expected or the length field in the protocol header would be incorrect. This could result in heap buffer overflows or malformed packets.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `orders` array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `orders` array is allocated with `new[]` before the checks on `header->insnum` and `header->patnum`. If either check fails, the function returns `MP_LOADER_FAILED` without calling `delete[] orders`, leaking the allocated memory. An attacker could craft a malformed S3M file to repeatedly trigger this leak, potentially causing resource exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write to `header->ord` array
vulnerabilities.long_description: `header->ordnum` is read from the file as a 16-bit word and can be up to 65535. The loop copies order entries into `header->ord[j++]` without checking whether `j` exceeds the fixed size of the `header->ord` array. A malicious S3M file with a large order count and no 0xFF sentinel values would write far beyond the bounds of the `ord` array, leading to heap or stack corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on sample index `s` against allocated `smp` array size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `s` is incremented each time a valid sample-type instrument is found, but it is never checked against the allocated size of `module->smp`. If a crafted file declares many instruments that are all valid samples, `s` could exceed the sample array bounds, causing out-of-bounds writes to `smp[s]` fields (name, flags, samplen, etc.) during instrument parsing and later during sample loading. This can lead to heap corruption and arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.

commit_message:
Save stack space while handling errors

Because error handling (luaG_errormsg) uses slots from EXTRA_STACK,
and some errors can recur (e.g., string overflow while creating an
error message in 'luaG_runerror', or a C-stack overflow before calling
the message handler), the code should use stack slots with parsimony.

This commit fixes the bug "Lua-stack overflow when C stack overflows
while handling an error".
cve_desc: An issue in the component luaG_runerror of Lua v5.4.4 and below leads to a heap-buffer overflow when a recursive error occurs.

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When k is large (but still <= Kmax), the expression `1 << k` can overflow a 32-bit int (undefined behavior for k >= 31), and the subsequent allocation size `sizeof(Bigint) + (x-1)*sizeof(Long)` can also wrap around. This could result in a small heap allocation while the code believes it has space for many more elements, leading to a heap buffer overflow when the Bigint is later used. The safety depends entirely on the value of Kmax, but if Kmax is >= 30, this is exploitable.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when copying order list entries into fixed-size header->ord array
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop copies order bytes from the file-sourced `orders` array into `header->ord[]` without checking that `j` stays within the bounds of the `ord` array (which is typically a fixed-size array, e.g., 256 entries). Since `header->ordnum` is read directly from the file, a crafted S3M file with a large ordnum value can cause `j` to exceed the fixed array size, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. This could lead to code execution or memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access on smp[] and instr[] arrays using file-controlled insnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values `header->insnum` and the derived sample index `s` are controlled by the input file. The code iterates `i` up to `header->insnum` and accesses `instr[i]` and `smp[s]` without validating that these indices stay within the bounds of the pre-allocated `module->instr` and `module->smp` arrays (which have fixed maximum sizes like MAX_INSTRUMENTS/MAX_SAMPLES). A malicious S3M file with a large instrument count can cause out-of-bounds reads and writes on the heap, leading to memory corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on phead[] array using file-controlled patnum
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value `header->patnum` is read directly from the file and used as the loop bound when iterating over patterns. The code accesses `phead[i]` without checking whether `i` exceeds the pre-allocated size of the `module->phead` array. A crafted file with a large pattern count will cause out-of-bounds heap access when calling `convertS3MPattern(&phead[i], ...)`, potentially leading to memory corruption and code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of orders array on early return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `orders` array is allocated early, but if the subsequent allocation of `insParaPtrs` fails (returns NULL), the function returns MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY without freeing the `orders` array first. While `orders` is freed in the normal path, the error handling path after `delete[] orders` but in the context of other early returns doesn't apply here since orders is already freed. However, looking more broadly, if an exception occurs or other error paths are hit between allocation of orders and its deletion, the memory would leak. This is a minor concern but represents incomplete error handling.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in ReadBlob size calculation from attacker-controlled dimensions
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `depth/8*image->columns` can overflow, especially on 32-bit platforms. `image->columns` is derived directly from `HDR.nRows` (an unsigned int read from the file, up to ~4 billion), and `depth/8` can be up to 8. If the multiplication wraps around, ReadBlob will read far fewer bytes than expected, while ImportQuantumPixels will process the full image width, potentially operating on uninitialized heap memory from the quantum pixel buffer. This could lead to information disclosure or unexpected behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return values of SetQuantumFormat/Depth/Endian are overwritten without checking, leading to use of misconfigured quantum info
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return status from SetQuantumFormat, SetQuantumDepth, and SetQuantumEndian are each assigned to the same `status` variable, overwriting the previous result. None of them are checked before proceeding. If any of these calls fail (e.g., SetQuantumDepth fails to reallocate the pixel buffer for the requested depth), the code continues to use a potentially misconfigured or undersized quantum pixel buffer in ReadBlob and ImportQuantumPixels, which could cause heap buffer overflows or out-of-bounds reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in seek offset calculation during ping mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ping (metadata-only) path, the seek offset is computed as `HDR.nCols*ldblk`. Both values are derived from file input. Since `HDR.nCols` is an unsigned int and `ldblk` is a long, their product can overflow, resulting in a seek to an incorrect position. This could cause the parser loop to misinterpret subsequent data as a new MAT4 header, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or other memory corruption when processing a crafted file.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

code_snippets:
old_len = (long)STRLEN(ptr);
new_line = alloc(old_len - col + start_col + len + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size `old_len - col + start_col + len + 1` is computed using `long` arithmetic. With a sufficiently long line, the sum `old_len + len + 1` could overflow a `long`, wrapping to a small or negative value. When cast to `size_t` for `alloc()`, this results in a too-small buffer allocation. The subsequent `mch_memmove` calls and tab/space filling loop then write beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control buffer contents (e.g., a crafted file opened in Vim) could potentially achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Implicit truncation from long to int when parsing tab stop value
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the non-FEAT_VARTABS code path, `getdigits()` returns a `long`, but the result is stored in `new_ts` which is an `int`. If the parsed number fits in a `long` but not an `int`, the value is silently truncated before the subsequent range checks (`new_ts < 0 || new_ts > TABSTOP_MAX`). Depending on the platform and the truncated value, this could bypass validation and lead to incorrect tabstop calculations, including potential division-by-zero or incorrect buffer size computations in the retabulation arithmetic (e.g., `start_vcol % new_ts` or `num_spaces / new_ts`).

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access when tool_num is outside valid range
vulnerabilities.long_description: When tool_num is less than TOOL_MIN or greater than or equal to TOOL_MAX, an error message is logged but execution continues. The code then uses tool_num as an index into image->aperture[tool_num] without returning or otherwise preventing further use. This leads to out-of-bounds read and potentially out-of-bounds write (when assigning image->aperture[tool_num] = g_new0(...) later). An attacker crafting a malicious drill file can trigger heap/stack corruption or arbitrary memory access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pointer arithmetic error: fd++ increments the struct pointer, corrupting subsequent accesses
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `get_line(fd++)` passes the current value of fd to get_line but then increments the fd pointer (which is a pointer to a gerb_file_t struct) by sizeof(gerb_file_t). All subsequent uses of fd in this function (e.g., fd->filename in the gerbv_stats_printf call immediately after, and all later operations) will reference memory past the original struct, leading to out-of-bounds memory access. This is almost certainly a typo (should be just `fd`), and a malicious file triggering the TCS path could cause crashes or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing closing brace causes newline check to be inside EOF block, and EOF condition doesn't break the loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: The closing brace of the EOF check block is placed after the newline check, meaning the newline restoration code is unreachable (temp cannot be both EOF and a newline). More critically, when EOF is encountered, the code does not set `done = TRUE` or break out of the while loop, causing an infinite loop reading EOF repeatedly. A truncated or malformed drill file would cause the program to hang indefinitely.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to potentially negative modulo result
vulnerabilities.long_description: In C, the modulo operator with a negative dividend produces a negative result. If (r + h - bh) is negative, y0 will be negative, causing the subsequent array index (y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw to be negative. This leads to an out-of-bounds read on lprn->ImageBuf, which could cause a crash or leak sensitive memory contents.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero if maxY is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: If lprn->BlockLine is smaller than lprn->nBh, the integer division lprn->BlockLine / lprn->nBh yields 0, making maxY = 0. The subsequent modulo operation (r + h - bh) % maxY would then be a division by zero, causing undefined behavior or a program crash. Similarly, if lprn->nBh is zero, both the division for maxY and the loop bound would be problematic.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read when scan range is smaller than 4 bytes due to unsigned integer underflow in comparison
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `to` is less than 4 (but greater than `from`), the expression `to - 4` undergoes unsigned integer underflow (since `to` is `ut64`), wrapping to a very large value. This makes the condition `needle < to - 4` always true, causing `r_read_le32` to read 4 bytes from `buf` even when the allocated buffer size (`to - from`) is less than 4 bytes. This results in a heap buffer over-read of up to 3 bytes. An attacker could craft a binary with specific section layouts to trigger this, potentially leaking adjacent heap memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4974: ":so" command may read after end of buffer

Problem:    ":so" command may read after end of buffer.
Solution:   Compute length of text properly.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4974.

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in
	hw_atl_utils_fw_upload_dwords+0x393/0x3c0 [atlantic]

Reported-by: Brendan Dolan-Gavitt <brendandg@nyu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Zekun Shen <bruceshenzk@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: In the Linux kernel through 5.15.2, hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait in drivers/net/ethernet/aquantia/atlantic/hw_atl/hw_atl_utils.c allows an attacker (who can introduce a crafted device) to trigger an out-of-bounds write via a crafted length value.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow causes infinite loop in error cleanup path
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `u` is declared as `unsigned int`. In the error-handling cleanup loop, after `u--` makes `u` the index of the last successfully allocated entry, the loop condition `while (u >= 0)` is always true for an unsigned integer. When `u` reaches 0 and is decremented, it wraps around to UINT_MAX (4294967295), causing the loop to continue indefinitely and access memory far out of bounds of the `ContribRow` array. This leads to heap out-of-bounds reads/frees and likely a crash or potentially exploitable memory corruption. An attacker who can trigger the allocation failure (e.g., by requesting a very large image resize) could exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of res and res->ContribRow in error cleanup path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the error cleanup path is triggered (allocation failure or overflow for Weights), the function frees the individual Weights allocations but never frees `res->ContribRow` or `res` itself before returning NULL. This results in a memory leak every time this error path is taken. Repeated triggering could lead to resource exhaustion.

patch 8.2.4974: ":so" command may read after end of buffer

commit_message:
sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk

sctp_make_strreset_req() makes repeated calls to sctp_addto_chunk()
which will automatically account for padding on each call. inreq and
outreq are already 4 bytes aligned, but the payload is not and doing
SCTP_PAD4(a + b) (which _sctp_make_chunk() did implicitly here) is
different from SCTP_PAD4(a) + SCTP_PAD4(b) and not enough. It led to
possible attempt to use more buffer than it was allocated and triggered
a BUG_ON.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>
Cc: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Cc: Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in stream_len calculation due to __u16 multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable stream_len is declared as __u16 (unsigned 16-bit), and stream_num is also __u16. The multiplication `stream_num * sizeof(__u16)` (i.e., stream_num * 2) can overflow the 16-bit range when stream_num > 32767. This would cause stream_len to wrap around to a small value, leading to an undersized buffer allocation via sctp_make_reconf, while sctp_addto_chunk could later write more data than expected based on the actual stream_list content, or conversely the param_hdr.length field would encode the truncated value causing protocol-level issues. An attacker able to control stream_num could exploit this for a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow when allocation fails at u=0 leads to massive out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the first iteration of the loop (u=0) encounters an overflow or allocation failure, the code executes `u--` on an unsigned int. This wraps `u` to UINT_MAX (4294967295). The subsequent cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` will then iterate up to UINT_MAX times, reading far beyond the bounds of `res->ContribRow` and calling `gdFree` on arbitrary memory contents. This can lead to heap corruption, crashes, or potentially arbitrary code execution. An attacker who can influence `windows_size` to trigger the overflow2 check or cause gdMalloc to fail on the very first iteration can exploit this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak: res->ContribRow is not freed in the error cleanup path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When allocation fails inside the loop, the error-handling code frees individual Weights and then frees `res`, but never frees `res->ContribRow` itself. This results in a memory leak of the `ContribRow` array each time this error path is taken.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4646: using buffer line after it has been freed

Problem:    Using buffer line after it has been freed in old regexp engine.
Solution:   After getting mark get the line again.
cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in MULTIBYTECODE case: rex.input advanced by len even on mismatch
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, after the byte-by-byte comparison loop, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally regardless of whether `status` was set to `RA_NOMATCH`. If the input string is shorter than `len` bytes (e.g., the comparison loop encounters a NUL byte before completing all `len` comparisons), the pointer `rex.input` is still advanced by the full `len`. This moves the input pointer past the NUL terminator of the string, leading to out-of-bounds reads in subsequent operations that dereference `rex.input`. The comparison loop itself may also read `rex.input[i]` past the NUL terminator if `opnd[i]` happens to also be NUL (unlikely for valid multibyte chars but possible with crafted regex programs). An attacker who can control the regex pattern and input could potentially use this to read sensitive data from memory beyond the input buffer.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow due to rounding up sample count when silencing remaining buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The silence-fill size is calculated by rounding UP the number of samples via `(8 * remaining_bytes + 7) / width`. When the remaining byte count is not aligned to the sample size (width/8), this rounding up produces a sample count whose total byte footprint (`size * width / 8`) exceeds the actual remaining space in `runtime->oss.buffer` (which is allocated as `period_bytes`). For example, with `buffer_used` leaving a remainder not aligned to the physical frame size and certain bit-widths, `snd_pcm_format_set_silence` will write past the end of the buffer, corrupting adjacent heap memory. An attacker controlling OSS parameters (format, write sizes) could trigger this to achieve heap corruption.

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

Reported-by: Brendan Dolan-Gavitt <brendandg@nyu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Zekun Shen <bruceshenzk@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: In the Linux kernel through 5.15.2, hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait in drivers/net/ethernet/aquantia/atlantic/hw_atl/hw_atl_utils.c allows an attacker (who can introduce a crafted device) to trigger an out-of-bounds write via a crafted length value.

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds validation on parameter 'k' leads to out-of-bounds array access and integer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameter 'k' is used directly as an index into the 'freelist' array without any bounds checking. A negative or excessively large value of 'k' could access memory outside the 'freelist' array bounds (buffer over-read/write). Additionally, '1 << k' causes undefined behavior if k is negative, >= the bit-width of int (typically 31 on 32-bit), and can produce unexpected values, leading to an undersized allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow when the Bigint is used.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on MALLOC return value leads to potential NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: If MALLOC fails and returns NULL, the code proceeds to dereference rv (rv->sign = rv->wds = 0) without any check. This results in a NULL pointer dereference, which can cause a crash (denial of service) or, on some platforms without memory protection at address 0, potentially exploitable memory corruption.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when processing CurveToElement with insufficient remaining points
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the path contains a CurveToElement, the code reads 6 qreal values (points[0] through points[5]) and advances the types pointer by 3. However, the loop condition only checks `points < lastPoint`, which merely guarantees at least 2 remaining values (one point), not 6. If a malformed or truncated path has a CurveToElement type near the end without two subsequent CurveToData elements and their associated point data, the code will read up to 4 qreal values past the end of the points buffer and up to 2 entries past the end of the types array. This occurs in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths. An attacker who can control the vector path data could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Infinite loop on unexpected element type due to missing pointer advancement in default case
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths, the switch statement over `*types` has a `default` case that simply breaks without advancing either the `points` or `types` pointers. If a path element has an unexpected or corrupted type value (not MoveTo, LineTo, or CurveTo), the loop condition `points < lastPoint` will never become false, resulting in an infinite loop. This can be triggered by malformed path data and leads to a denial of service by hanging the application.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in StrokeHandler allocation size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `path.elementCount()+4` could overflow if `elementCount()` returns a value near the maximum of its integer type. This could result in allocating a much smaller buffer than intended, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when the stroke handler processes the path elements. While exploitation depends on whether an attacker can control the element count to be near INT_MAX, the lack of an overflow check makes this a potential memory corruption vulnerability.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in MTU calculation allows setting an arbitrarily large MTU on the tunnel device
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG handler, `mtu` is declared as `__u32` (unsigned) and computed as `be32_to_cpu(info) - offset`. If `be32_to_cpu(info)` is less than `offset`, the subtraction wraps around to a very large unsigned value (close to UINT32_MAX). The subsequent guard `if (mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU)` will not trigger because the wrapped value far exceeds IPV6_MIN_MTU. This results in `t->dev->mtu` being set to an enormous value. An attacker who can send crafted ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages with a small MTU value in `info` can exploit this to set an invalid, extremely large MTU on the tunnel device, potentially leading to memory corruption or oversized allocations when transmitting through the tunnel. Additionally, the MTU is set directly without any upper-bound validation, bypassing normal netdevice MTU change checks.

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pointer arithmetic on struct pointer causes out-of-bounds memory access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `fd++` performs a post-increment on the `gerb_file_t*` pointer. After `get_line` is called with the original pointer value, `fd` is advanced to point past the actual struct into invalid memory. All subsequent uses of `fd` in the function (e.g., `fd->filename`, `gerb_fgetc(fd)`, `gerb_ungetc(fd)`, `gerb_fgetint(fd, ...)`) will dereference this corrupted pointer, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes. This can cause crashes, information disclosure, or potentially arbitrary code execution. This code path is reached when the input contains a 'TCST' sequence.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Misplaced brace causes newline restoration logic to be dead code inside EOF check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `if ('\n' == temp || '\r' == temp)` check is placed inside the `if (EOF == temp)` block due to incorrect brace placement. Since `temp` cannot simultaneously be EOF and a newline character, `gerb_ungetc(fd)` is never called when a newline or carriage return is encountered. This means the while loop does not properly restore newline/carriage-return characters, causing the loop to consume and skip over line terminators. This can lead to incorrect parsing of subsequent data, and since the loop continues executing after detecting EOF (the `done` flag is not set and there is no `break`), it results in an infinite loop on EOF as `gerb_fgetc` will keep returning EOF.

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition: priv->free checked without lock, then dereferenced under lock
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `if (!priv->free)` is performed without holding the spinlock, but the subsequent dereference `buffer = priv->free` and `priv->free = buffer->next` happen under the lock. Between the check and acquiring the lock, another thread/context could consume the last free buffer, making priv->free NULL. This leads to a NULL pointer dereference when `buffer->next` is accessed. An attacker with concurrent write access to the device could trigger a kernel NULL pointer dereference, causing a denial of service (kernel panic/oops).

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4206: condition with many "(" causes a crash

Problem:    Condition with many "(" causes a crash.
Solution:   Limit recursion to 1000.
cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4206: condition with many "(" causes a crash

Problem:    Condition with many "(" causes a crash.
Solution:   Limit recursion to 1000.
cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

commit_message: check max member count in class
cve_desc: sqclass.cpp in Squirrel through 2.2.5 and 3.x through 3.1 allows an out-of-bounds read (in the core interpreter) that can lead to Code Execution. If a victim executes an attacker-controlled squirrel script, it is possible for the attacker to break out of the squirrel script sandbox even if all dangerous functionality such as File System functions has been disabled. An attacker might abuse this bug to target (for example) Cloud services that allow customization via SquirrelScripts, or distribute malware through video games that embed a Squirrel Engine.

commit_message:
bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf

A BPF program might try to reserve a buffer larger than the ringbuf size.
If the consumer pointer is way ahead of the producer, that would be
successfully reserved, allowing the BPF program to read or write out of
the ringbuf allocated area.

Reported-by: Ryota Shiga (Flatt Security)
Fixes: 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it")
Signed-off-by: Thadeu Lima de Souza Cascardo <cascardo@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Andrii Nakryiko <andrii@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
cve_desc: The eBPF RINGBUF bpf_ringbuf_reserve() function in the Linux kernel did not check that the allocated size was smaller than the ringbuf size, allowing an attacker to perform out-of-bounds writes within the kernel and therefore, arbitrary code execution. This issue was fixed via commit 4b81ccebaeee ("bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf") (v5.13-rc4) and backported to the stable kernels in v5.12.4, v5.11.21, and v5.10.37. It was introduced via 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it") (v5.8-rc1).

commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: g_free called on potentially uninitialized s->catalog_bitmap pointer on early failure paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: If bdrv_pread fails on its first call (reading the header) or any check fails before g_malloc assigns to s->catalog_bitmap, the code jumps to the 'fail' label and calls g_free(s->catalog_bitmap). If the opaque structure was not zeroed at allocation time, s->catalog_bitmap contains an indeterminate value, meaning g_free is called on a garbage pointer. This can lead to heap corruption, a crash, or potentially exploitable behavior. The fix would be to initialize s->catalog_bitmap = NULL at the start of the function, or to restructure the error handling so g_free is only called after the allocation has occurred.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when processing CurveToElement with insufficient remaining points
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the element type is CurveToElement, the code reads 6 qreal values (points[0] through points[5]) and advances the points pointer by 6 and types pointer by 3. However, the loop condition only checks `points < lastPoint`, which is true if even a single point remains. If a malformed QVectorPath has a CurveToElement type near the end of the path data without at least 6 remaining qreal values (3 points), this results in an out-of-bounds heap read. This occurs in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths. An attacker able to influence path data could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Infinite loop when an unrecognized element type is encountered
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths, the switch statement over `*types` has a default case that does nothing — it neither advances the `points` pointer nor the `types` pointer. If the types array contains an unexpected or corrupted value that doesn't match MoveToElement, LineToElement, or CurveToElement, the loop condition `points < lastPoint` remains true forever, causing an infinite loop and denial of service. A malformed QVectorPath with an invalid element type would trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in StrokeHandler allocation size
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `path.elementCount()` returns a very large value close to INT_MAX, adding 4 could cause an integer overflow. Depending on how StrokeHandler uses this size parameter for internal buffer allocation, this could result in a much smaller buffer being allocated than expected, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when stroke data is written into the undersized buffer.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Negative modulo result can cause out-of-bounds access on ImageBuf
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `r + h - bh` evaluates to a negative value, the C modulo operator `%` can return a negative result, making `y0` negative. This negative `y0` is then used to index into `lprn->ImageBuf` via `(y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw`, producing a negative (or otherwise out-of-bounds) offset. This leads to reading memory before the start of the ImageBuf buffer. An attacker who can influence the values of `r`, `h`, or the device parameters could trigger this to read or potentially corrupt memory outside the allocated buffer, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential division by zero if nBh is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `lprn->nBh` is zero, the computation of `maxY` involves a division by zero (`lprn->BlockLine / lprn->nBh`). Even if that were somehow avoided, `maxY` could be zero, and the subsequent modulo operation `(r + h - bh) % maxY` would also be a division by zero. There is no validation that `nBh` is positive before use, so a misconfigured or malicious device state could cause a crash.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap/stack buffer over-read due to unchecked reason length against packet size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code reads the BYE reason length from the byte at offset 8 in the packet and clamps it only against the destination buffer size (peer_sdes_buf_), but never validates it against the actual remaining packet data (size - 9). An attacker can craft a packet where the length byte at offset 8 is larger than the remaining bytes in the packet, causing pj_memcpy to read beyond the end of the packet buffer. This can lead to information disclosure or a crash. The fix should also clamp reason.slen to (size - 9).

commit_message:
Fix heap buffer overflow in stl_update_connects_remove_1


- Add argument value check to the stl_update_connects_remove_1
- Add neighbor value check in stl_remove_degenerate

Fixes https://github.com/admesh/admesh/issues/28
Merges https://github.com/admesh/admesh/pull/55
cve_desc: ADMesh through 0.98.4 has a heap-based buffer over-read in stl_update_connects_remove_1 (called from stl_remove_degenerate) in connect.c in libadmesh.a.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer over-read in pattern data decompression due to missing bounds check on `pc`
vulnerabilities.long_description: When decompressing pattern data, the index variable `pc` is incremented based on the contents of the buffer (compressed pattern data) without any check that `pc` remains within the bounds of the allocated `buffer` (of size `phead[y].patdata`). A malformed XM file can craft pattern data such that the decompression loop reads well beyond the end of the buffer. This can lead to information disclosure or a crash. An attacker can supply a pattern where `patdata` (the compressed size) is small but indicates many rows/channels, or where the compressed flags cause many bytes to be consumed, causing `pc` to exceed the buffer size.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized `smpReloc` array when indexed by file-controlled `nbu` values in v0x102/0x103 path
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the version 0x102/0x103 instrument loading path, `smpReloc` is a stack-allocated array that is only partially initialized for indices 0 through `instr[y].samp-1`. However, `nbu` values (which are read directly from the file and can be 0-255) are used to index into `smpReloc`. If `nbu[sc]` is greater than or equal to the number of samples actually processed, the code reads uninitialized stack memory. This can lead to use of garbage values for `instr[y].snum[sc]`, potentially causing out-of-bounds accesses later when the sample numbers are used, or information leakage.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pattern data allocation size calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `phead[y].rows * header->channum * 6` is computed without overflow checking. Both `phead[y].rows` (a 16-bit word read from file) and `header->channum` (also read from file) are attacker-controlled. If their product overflows, a small buffer will be allocated but a larger (or also overflowed) size will be used in the subsequent `memset` and data writes (`bc` index), leading to a heap buffer overflow. For example, large values of rows and channum could cause the allocation to be very small while the loop writes far more data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in MODPLUG text extension length calculation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When parsing the MODPLUG 'text' extension, `len` is read as a 32-bit unsigned integer from the file. The expression `len+1` can overflow to 0 if `len` is `0xFFFFFFFF`. This would cause `allocateSongMessage(0)` to allocate a tiny (or zero-size) buffer, while `memset` with size 0 would be benign but the subsequent `f.read(module->message, 1, len)` would attempt to read ~4GB into the undersized buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
drivers: hamradio: 6pack: fix UAF bug caused by mod_timer()

When a 6pack device is detaching, the sixpack_close() will act to cleanup
necessary resources. Although del_timer_sync() in sixpack_close()
won't return if there is an active timer, one could use mod_timer() in
sp_xmit_on_air() to wake up timer again by calling userspace syscall such
as ax25_sendmsg(), ax25_connect() and ax25_ioctl().

This unexpected waked handler, sp_xmit_on_air(), realizes nothing about
the undergoing cleanup and may still call pty_write() to use driver layer
resources that have already been released.

One of the possible race conditions is shown below:

      (USE)                      |      (FREE)
ax25_sendmsg()                   |
 ax25_queue_xmit()               |
  ...                            |
  sp_xmit()                      |
   sp_encaps()                   | sixpack_close()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |  del_timer_sync(&sp->tx_t)
     mod_timer(&sp->tx_t,...)    |  ...
                                 |  unregister_netdev()
                                 |  ...
     (wait a while)              | tty_release()
                                 |  tty_release_struct()
                                 |   release_tty()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |    tty_kref_put(tty_struct) //FREE
     pty_write(tty_struct) //USE |    ...

The corresponding fail log is shown below:
===============================================================
BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in __run_timers.part.0+0x170/0x470
Write of size 8 at addr ffff88800a652ab8 by task swapper/2/0
...
Call Trace:
  ...
  queue_work_on+0x3f/0x50
  pty_write+0xcd/0xe0pty_write+0xcd/0xe0
  sp_xmit_on_air+0xb2/0x1f0
  call_timer_fn+0x28/0x150
  __run_timers.part.0+0x3c2/0x470
  run_timer_softirq+0x3b/0x80
  __do_softirq+0xf1/0x380
  ...

This patch reorders the del_timer_sync() after the unregister_netdev()
to avoid UAF bugs. Because the unregister_netdev() is well synchronized,
it flushs out any pending queues, waits the refcount of net_device
decreases to zero and removes net_device from kernel. There is not any
running routines after executing unregister_netdev(). Therefore, we could
not arouse timer from userspace again.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Reviewed-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerabilitity was discovered in drivers/net/hamradio/6pack.c of linux that allows an attacker to crash linux kernel by simulating ax25 device using 6pack driver from user space.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5123: using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions

Problem:    Using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions.
Solution:   Do not decrement the index when it is zero.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap/stack buffer overflow when REP item replacement is longer than the original in fword[]
vulnerabilities.long_description: In STATE_REP, when a REP rule's 'to' string is longer than its 'from' string (tl > fl), STRMOVE shifts the remainder of fword[] to the right to make room. However, there is no check that the expanded content still fits within fword[]'s allocated buffer (MAXWLEN). Multiple REP replacements that each increase the length (tracked by repextra) can cause fword[] to grow beyond its buffer boundary, resulting in a stack or heap buffer overflow depending on how fword is allocated by the caller. An attacker who can influence the spell dictionary's REP rules and the input bad word could trigger arbitrary memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to stack buffer tword[] when ts_twordlen reaches MAXWLEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: In STATE_PLAIN and STATE_INS, bytes are appended to the stack-allocated tword[MAXWLEN] buffer by writing tword[sp->ts_twordlen++] = c. While TRY_DEEPER checks depth against MAXWLEN, there is no explicit check that ts_twordlen stays within MAXWLEN before the write. Since ts_twordlen can be incremented independently through multiple insertion operations at the same depth, it is possible for ts_twordlen to reach or exceed MAXWLEN, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow in preword[] through repeated compound/split word concatenation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The preword[] buffer is sized at MAXWLEN * 3, but multiple operations append to it without verifying the total length stays within bounds. Through repeated word splitting (each appending a space and new word segment via STRCAT/STRCPY), compound word concatenation via vim_strncpy, and make_case_word calls, the accumulated content in preword[] can exceed its allocated size. An attacker providing a crafted spell dictionary with compound rules and a sufficiently long bad word could trigger a stack buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution.

code_snippets:
sprintf(changename[depth], "%.*s-%s: split",
				 sp->ts_twordlen, tword, fword + sp->ts_fidx);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow in debug changename[][] via sprintf with unchecked format arguments
vulnerabilities.long_description: When DEBUG_TRIEWALK is defined, numerous sprintf calls write into changename[depth] which is only 80 bytes wide. The format strings include variable-length data from tword (up to MAXWLEN bytes), fword (variable length), and REP item from/to strings. Since these can easily exceed 80 bytes combined, a stack buffer overflow occurs. While this is debug-only code, if a debug build is deployed, a malicious spell dictionary or input could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

      (USE)                      |      (FREE)
ax25_sendmsg()                   |

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race on priv->free can lead to NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `if (!priv->free)` is performed without holding the spinlock. After `pxa3xx_gcu_wait_free` returns (or if the check passes because `priv->free` is non-NULL), another thread or context could consume all free buffers before the spinlock is acquired. Inside the locked section, `buffer = priv->free` could be NULL, and the subsequent `buffer->next` dereference would cause a kernel NULL pointer dereference (oops/panic). An attacker with access to the device node could trigger this by issuing concurrent writes.

commit_message:
ovl: prevent private clone if bind mount is not allowed

Add the following checks from __do_loopback() to clone_private_mount() as
well:

 - verify that the mount is in the current namespace

 - verify that there are no locked children

Reported-by: Alois Wohlschlager <alois1@gmx-topmail.de>
Fixes: c771d683a62e ("vfs: introduce clone_private_mount()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v3.18
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Linux kernel's OverlayFS subsystem in the way the user mounts the TmpFS filesystem with OverlayFS. This flaw allows a local user to gain access to hidden files that should not be accessible.

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when the first realloc fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `realloc(result, ...)` is called for the first time, `result` is NULL. If `realloc` returns NULL (allocation failure), the condition `!tmp && result` evaluates to false (because `result` is also NULL). The code then falls through, sets `result = tmp` (NULL), and dereferences it with `result[i++] = dupstr(cptr)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference. This can lead to a crash (denial of service) or, on some systems, potentially exploitable undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in pattern data buffer due to missing bounds check on 'pc' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: When parsing pattern data, the variable 'pc' is used to index into 'buffer' which has size 'phead[y].patdata'. However, 'pc' is never validated against the buffer size before each access. A crafted XM file can specify pattern data that causes the parsing loop (iterating over rows and channels) to read past the end of the allocated buffer. This leads to heap buffer over-read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when instrument size is less than 4 leads to huge read into stack buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the v0x104 instrument loading path, when 'instr[y].size < 29', the code computes 'instr[y].size - 4' as the number of bytes to read. Since instr[y].size is an unsigned 32-bit integer read from the file, if it is 0, 1, 2, or 3, the subtraction wraps around to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFC). This causes f.read to attempt to read a huge amount of data into a 29-byte stack buffer, resulting in a stack buffer overflow that could allow arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized smpReloc array entries when nbu values reference indices beyond loaded samples
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the v0x102/v0x103 instrument loading path, the 'smpReloc' array is stack-allocated without initialization. Only entries from index 0 to instr[y].samp-1 are populated during sample loading. However, 'nbu' values (read from file data) can be any value from 0 to MP_MAXINSSAMPS-1. When the loop accesses 'smpReloc[nbu[sc]]' where nbu[sc] >= instr[y].samp, it reads uninitialized stack memory, leading to unpredictable behavior and potential information disclosure via the resulting sample number assignments.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to smp array: bounds check on 's' occurs after writes to smp[g+s]
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code writes to 'smp[g+s]' (setting flags, envelope numbers, reading sample headers from file, etc.) before checking whether 's' has exceeded MP_MAXSAMPLES. Since 'g' can be > 0 when multiple samples exist per instrument, the index 'g+s' can exceed the array bounds before the check 's>=MP_MAXSAMPLES' is reached. A crafted XM file with many instruments/samples can trigger heap buffer overflow on the smp array, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in pattern data allocation size computation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size 'phead[y].rows * header->channum * 6' is computed using values read from the file. Since phead[y].rows is a 16-bit value (up to 65535) and header->channum is also file-controlled, their product multiplied by 6 can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequent memset and pattern data writes would then overflow the heap buffer, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error allows out-of-bounds access to fixed file table
vulnerabilities.long_description: The boundary check uses `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` instead of `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`. Since `nr_user_files` represents the count of user files (indexed 0 to nr_user_files-1), an `fd` value equal to `nr_user_files` passes this check but results in an out-of-bounds access when used in `io_fixed_file_slot(&ctx->file_table, fd)`. An attacker with access to the io_uring interface could pass an fd equal to `nr_user_files` to read memory one slot beyond the allocated file table, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing memory corruption. Note that `array_index_nospec` mitigates speculative execution but does not prevent the architectural out-of-bounds access since the bound passed to it (`ctx->nr_user_files`) also does not account for the off-by-one.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient image dimension validation allows integer overflow or bypass of size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sanity check divides the product of columns and rows by 8, which makes it extremely lenient. Attackers can craft a CIN file with very large image->columns and image->rows values (read directly from the file as cin.image.channel[0].pixels_per_line and cin.image.channel[0].lines_per_image) that pass this check but then cause excessive memory allocation or out-of-bounds writes when processing pixel rows. For example, columns=0xFFFFFFFF and rows=1 would yield a product/8 that could appear small, but the actual memory needed for GetBytesPerRow and pixel processing could be enormous or cause integer overflows in downstream calculations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled user_length can cause excessive memory allocation when blob size is unknown
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cin.file.user_length value is read directly from the file and used to allocate memory via BlobToStringInfo. The guard check compares it against GetBlobSize(image), but for non-seekable streams (pipes, stdin), GetBlobSize may return 0, causing the comparison to always pass. An attacker could set user_length to a very large value (up to ~4GB since it's a 32-bit field from ReadBlobLong), causing a denial of service through excessive memory allocation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated image depth from file input could cause undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: image->depth is set directly from cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel (a single byte read from the file) without any validation that the value is a supported bit depth (e.g., 8, 10, 12, 16). A value of 0 or an unexpected value could cause division-by-zero or incorrect calculations in GetBytesPerRow and ImportQuantumPixels, potentially leading to buffer overflows or other memory corruption during pixel row processing.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when skb has zero length data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function dereferences skb->data to read the packet type byte without first verifying that the skb contains at least 1 byte of data (i.e., skb->len >= 1). If a malicious or malformed virtio device provides an empty skb, this results in reading memory beyond the valid buffer. An attacker controlling the virtio backend could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when packet type does not match any known type
vulnerabilities.long_description: When pkt_type does not match any of the handled cases (HCI_EVENT_PKT, HCI_ACLDATA_PKT, HCI_SCODATA_PKT, HCI_ISODATA_PKT), the switch statement falls through without freeing the skb. This means the sk_buff is never freed, resulting in a kernel memory leak. A malicious virtio backend could repeatedly send packets with unrecognized type values to exhaust kernel memory, leading to a denial of service.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0011: reading beyond the end of the line with put command

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of the line with put command.
Solution:   Adjust the end mark position.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

code_snippets:
totlen = count * (yanklen + spaces) + bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces;
	    newp = alloc(totlen + oldlen + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in block mode allocation size allows heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the MBLOCK handling code, the overflow check validates that `totlen` fits in INT_MAX, but does not check whether `totlen + oldlen + 1` overflows. Both `totlen` and `oldlen` are `int`. If their sum exceeds INT_MAX, the result wraps around to a small positive value, causing `alloc()` to allocate a small buffer. Subsequent `mch_memmove` calls then write far beyond the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the register contents and the line length could exploit this for code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in MCHAR single-line put allocation allows heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the MCHAR y_size==1 path, `totlen` is checked against INT_MAX via `count > INT_MAX / yanklen`, but the allocation `alloc(totlen + oldlen + 1)` does not verify the sum doesn't overflow. If `totlen` and `oldlen` are both large (but individually within INT_MAX range), their sum plus 1 can wrap around, allocating a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent memmove and copy operations then write past the end of the allocated buffer, causing heap corruption exploitable for arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in MCHAR multi-line split allocation allows heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When putting multi-line MCHAR content, the code computes `alloc(STRLEN(ptr) + totlen + 1)` without checking for overflow. If `STRLEN(ptr)` and `totlen` are both very large values, their sum can overflow the `size_t` or intermediate integer type, resulting in a small allocation. The subsequent STRCPY/STRCAT then writes beyond the buffer. An attacker controlling register contents and buffer line contents could trigger this for heap corruption and potential code execution.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix uninitialized field in rw io_kiocb

io_rw_init_file does not initialize kiocb->private, so when iocb_bio_iopoll
reads kiocb->private it can contain uninitialized data.

Fixes: 3e08773c3841 ("block: switch polling to be bio based")
Signed-off-by: Joseph Ravichandran <jravi@mit.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.17.5. io_rw_init_file in fs/io_uring.c lacks initialization of kiocb->private.

commit_message:

io_rw_init_file does not initialize kiocb->private, so when iocb_bio_iopoll
reads kiocb->private it can contain uninitialized data.

Fixes: 3e08773c3841 ("block: switch polling to be bio based")

commit_message:
fuse: fix live lock in fuse_iget()

Commit 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode") replaced make_bad_inode()
in fuse_iget() with a private implementation fuse_make_bad().

The private implementation fails to remove the bad inode from inode
cache, so the retry loop with iget5_locked() finds the same bad inode
and marks it bad forever.

kmsg snip:

[ ] rcu: INFO: rcu_sched self-detected stall on CPU
...
[ ]  ? bit_wait_io+0x50/0x50
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? find_inode.isra.32+0x60/0xb0
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5_nowait+0x65/0x90
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5.part.36+0x2e/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  iget5_locked+0x21/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20

Fixes: 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode")
