Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode() is stored in *i and then immediately dereferenced to access fields like mode, uid, gid, time, xattr, data, start, and offset without any NULL check. If read_inode() fails (e.g., due to a corrupted or malicious filesystem image) and returns NULL, this will cause a NULL pointer dereference, leading to a crash (denial of service) or potentially exploitable behavior depending on the platform.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The value maxVertices is derived from untrusted input (groupCode 90). The expression `4*maxVertices` can overflow a 32-bit integer (if maxVertices is large, e.g., greater than INT_MAX/4), resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. Subsequent initialization and vertex writes then overflow the undersized heap buffer, enabling arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when vertexIndex is -1 for groupCode 42
vulnerabilities.long_description: When groupCode is 42, the code checks `vertexIndex<maxVertices` but does not check `vertexIndex>=0`. Since vertexIndex is initialized to -1 and is only incremented when groupCode==10, a groupCode 42 record arriving before any groupCode 10 record will write to `vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1]`, causing a write before the allocated buffer. This is an out-of-bounds heap write that can corrupt heap metadata or adjacent objects, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow when formatting PID into fixed-size filename buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer is allocated as a duplicate of "/tmp/sd000000" (13 chars + null = 14 bytes). The sprintf writes the PID at offset 7, leaving only 7 bytes for the PID string and null terminator. On systems where PIDs can be large (e.g., up to 4194304 on Linux with pid_max set high), the PID string can exceed 6 characters, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via EDITOR environment variable passed to system()
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the EDITOR environment variable contains a space, the code constructs a command string and passes it to system(). A malicious user can set EDITOR to something like "vi; rm -rf /" or use shell metacharacters (backticks, $(), pipes, etc.) to inject arbitrary commands. The system() call invokes a shell which interprets these metacharacters, leading to arbitrary command execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insecure temporary file creation vulnerable to symlink/race attacks
vulnerabilities.long_description: The temporary file is created in /tmp with a predictable name based on the PID. An attacker can predict the filename and create a symlink at that path before the function runs, causing fopen to write to an arbitrary file (symlink attack / TOCTOU race condition). This could lead to overwriting sensitive files with attacker-influenced content.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of duplicated editor string
vulnerabilities.long_description: The editor string is duplicated with omStrDup but is never freed in any code path. This results in a memory leak each time sdb_edit is called. While not directly exploitable for code execution, repeated calls could lead to memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing cleanup of filename when language check fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When pi->language is not LANG_SINGULAR, the file is closed and fp set to NULL, but the code falls through to si_unlink(filename) and omFree(filename) at the end. However, the file was already created and written to /tmp before this check. More critically, the file remains on disk briefly with no content protection. The filename memory is freed, but the logic flow is confusing and the temporary file is created unnecessarily.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation for negative x and y coordinates leads to out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function clamps x and y when they exceed the image dimensions, but never checks if x or y are negative. If a negative value is passed for x or y, the subsequent calls to gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel will operate on out-of-bounds memory. For example, the loop `for (i = x; i >= 0; i--)` starts from a negative index if x < 0, and `gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y)` with a negative y will access memory before the pixel buffer. This can lead to heap buffer over-read or over-write, potentially enabling information disclosure or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uncontrolled recursion can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses recursive calls to fill rows above and below the current row. On a large image or with a pathological pattern of pixels, the recursion depth can grow proportionally to the image height (or even larger), exhausting the call stack. An attacker who controls the image content and the fill parameters can craft an image that triggers deep recursion, causing a stack overflow and denial of service (crash).

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed integer overflow in `pos` can lead to undefined behavior and bypass of bounds checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `pos` (an `int`) can be repeatedly decremented via `pos -= c - 10` (subtracting 1-9 each time) or `pos--` without intermediate bounds checking. With a crafted `rule` input containing many consecutive bytes in the range (11,19), `pos` could underflow past `INT_MIN`, triggering signed integer overflow which is undefined behavior in C. A compiler may optimize away the subsequent `pos < 0` bounds check under the assumption that signed overflow does not occur, potentially allowing out-of-bounds memory access via `decoded + pos` in the `memmove` calls, leading to arbitrary memory read/write.

code_snippets:
const int l = *decoded_size - pos;
if (pos < 0 || l < 0 || s + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read in delete branch when `pos` equals `*decoded_size`
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the delete branch (mod == 'd') when `dir` is '>' (so `pos` is not decremented), `pos` can equal `*decoded_size`. In this case, `l = *decoded_size - pos = 0`, which passes the `l < 0` check, and `s + l` also passes the buffer max check. However, `decoded[pos]` then reads one byte past the valid data region (at index `*decoded_size`). While this is likely within the allocated buffer, it reads stale or uninitialized data, and could lead to incorrect program behavior or information disclosure.

code_snippets: debug_print("Out of buffer in %s at pos: %i\n", decoded, pos);
debug_print("Character mismatch in %s at pos: %i (%c != %c)\n", decoded, pos, decoded[pos], c);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read via `%s` format on non-null-terminated buffer in debug_print
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `decoded` buffer is an `unsigned char*` that is not guaranteed to be null-terminated. When `debug_print` is called with `%s` formatting, it will read from `decoded` until a null byte is found, potentially reading far past the buffer boundary (`INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX`). This can lead to an out-of-bounds read causing a crash or information disclosure. An attacker providing a crafted rule that triggers an error condition could exploit this to leak memory contents if debug output is accessible.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode() is stored in *i but never checked for NULL before being dereferenced to access fields like mode, uid, gid, time, xattr, start, offset, and data. If read_inode fails (e.g., due to a corrupted filesystem image), it may return NULL, leading to a null pointer dereference and a crash. An attacker providing a crafted squashfs image could trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in directory size calculation leading to excessive loop iterations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'size' is computed as (*i)->data + bytes - 3, where (*i)->data comes from the parsed inode (filesystem-controlled input). If (*i)->data is a very large value (e.g., close to INT_MAX), the subtraction of 3 still leaves a very large 'size', causing the while loop to process far more data than a legitimate directory would contain. Combined with potential issues in read_directory_data, this could lead to processing of attacker-controlled data well beyond actual directory boundaries, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads or other memory corruption.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Reference count overflow on chain->use allows use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: The chain->use reference counter is incremented without any overflow check. An attacker in a position to repeatedly create rules with NFT_JUMP/NFT_GOTO verdicts referencing the same chain can overflow the use counter (e.g., wrapping a u32 back to 0). Once overflowed, the reference count no longer accurately tracks usage, allowing the chain to be freed while still referenced by verdict data, leading to a use-after-free condition. This is a known class of vulnerability in nftables reference counting (related to CVE-2022-32250) and can result in privilege escalation.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free via dangling interface data pointer after device free on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: After usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) is called, several error paths jump to err_free which frees 'dev' via kfree(dev) but never calls usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL). This leaves a dangling pointer stored in the USB interface's driver data. Any subsequent access to this interface data (e.g., during disconnect or other USB core callbacks) will dereference freed memory, potentially leading to code execution or kernel crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient cleanup on error after em28xx_init_dev succeeds, leading to resource leak and potential use-after-free
vulnerabilities.long_description: When em28xx_init_dev(dev, ...) succeeds but a later operation fails (e.g., the second em28xx_init_dev for dev_next in dual-tuner setup, or the speed check after init), the error path err_free only calls kfree on the device struct without undoing the initialization performed by em28xx_init_dev. This function likely registers sub-devices, allocates additional resources, and sets up callbacks. Simply freeing 'dev' without proper teardown leaves registered callbacks pointing to freed memory and leaks all resources allocated during initialization. Similarly, if the second em28xx_init_dev fails, the first initialized device is not properly torn down, and dev_next (allocated by em28xx_duplicate_dev) is leaked.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed as an `int` multiplication. If `maxVertices` (parsed from user-controlled input via `toInt(groupValue)`) is large enough (e.g., greater than INT_MAX/4), the multiplication overflows, resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. The subsequent initialization loop and later vertex writes use the original `maxVertices` value, causing massive out-of-bounds heap writes. An attacker can craft a DXF file with a group code 90 value that triggers this overflow to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds write due to unchecked groupCode arithmetic used as index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The index offset `(groupCode/10-1)` is derived from groupCode values 10, 20, or 30, yielding offsets 0, 1, or 2 respectively. While the current condition `groupCode<=30` constrains this, there is no explicit validation that groupCode is exactly 10, 20, or 30. If the guard condition were ever loosened or groupCode took an unexpected value within the range (e.g., values like 1-9 would yield `10/10-1=0` but could mask logic errors), the index calculation could be incorrect. This is a minor concern compared to the integer overflow but worth noting for defense-in-depth.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow via unchecked strcpy into fixed-size sun_path buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The pipePath string is copied into local.sun_path (typically 108 bytes on Linux) using strcpy with no length check. If pipePath exceeds the size of sun_path, this causes a stack buffer overflow, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on palette array in packed pixels mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the packed pixels decoding path, the computed `index` is masked to fit `bppp` bits and ANDed with 127, but it is never validated against `palSize`. For example, if `palSize` is 5, `bppp` will be 4, so `index` can be 0-15, but the palette only has 5 valid entries (indices 0-4). Reading `palette[index]` with index 5-15 reads uninitialized stack memory from the `palette[128]` array. A malicious server can craft ZRLE data to leak stack contents or cause unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on palette array in palette RLE mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the palette RLE decoding path, `index` is read from the stream, masked with `& 127`, and used directly to index into `palette[128]` without checking that `index < palSize`. A malicious server can send an index value that is within 0-127 but exceeds the actual palette size, causing a read of uninitialized stack data. This could lead to information disclosure or use of attacker-influenced pixel values.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0218: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.

cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in loop counter when stack_rev is true
vulnerabilities.long_description: When stack_rev is true, the loop variable `i` (of type `size_t`, which is unsigned) is decremented. If `to` is 0, the loop condition is `i >= 0` which is always true for an unsigned type. When `i` reaches 0 and is decremented, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX, satisfying `i >= to` (0), causing a near-infinite loop. This can lead to denial of service (hang/excessive CPU usage) and out-of-bounds reads from the type database functions called within the loop body.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when read_inode returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of read_inode() is stored in *i and immediately dereferenced to access members (mode, uid, gid, time, xattr) without any NULL check. If read_inode() fails and returns NULL (e.g., due to a corrupted filesystem image), this results in a NULL pointer dereference, causing a crash. An attacker crafting a malicious squashfs image could trigger this by providing invalid block_start/offset values that cause read_inode to fail.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in directory size calculation from untrusted inode data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'size' is computed from (*i)->data, which comes from the parsed (potentially attacker-controlled) filesystem inode. If (*i)->data is a very large value (close to INT_MAX), the arithmetic 'data + bytes - 3' could behave unexpectedly, or if data is less than 3, 'size' becomes negative and the loop is skipped (which may be benign). However, since 'bytes' is accumulated during the loop and checked against 'size', a very large 'size' derived from a crafted filesystem could cause the loop to read far beyond the actual directory data, processing attacker-controlled content as directory entries.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via strchr on non-null-terminated buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies `nbytes` bytes from `buf` into `drc_name` using `memcpy`, which does not append a null terminator. It then calls `strchr(drc_name, '\n')`, which scans memory until it finds the target character or a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is a stack-allocated buffer (`char drc_name[MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN]`) that is not initialized and not null-terminated after the `memcpy`, `strchr` may read beyond the `nbytes` copied bytes into uninitialized stack memory. This is an out-of-bounds read that could leak sensitive kernel stack data or cause undefined behavior. The fix is to null-terminate `drc_name` before calling `strchr`, e.g., `drc_name[nbytes] = '\0';`.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

patch 9.0.0218: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   When displaying "$" check the column is not negative.
cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost

leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of palette array in packed pixels decoding
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the packed pixels branch, the computed index is masked to fit the bits-per-pixel width and ANDed with 127, but it is never checked against palSize. For example, if palSize is 4, bppp is 2, and the attacker crafts the stream so that index is 3, that's fine. However if palSize is 3, valid indices are 0-2 but index 3 is possible. More critically, when palSize > 4 (bppp=4), index can range 0-15 but palSize could be 5, allowing reads of uninitialized stack memory from palette[5] through palette[15]. An attacker controlling the compressed stream can trigger reads of uninitialized stack data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of palette array in palette RLE decoding
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the palette RLE branch, the index read from the stream is masked with 127, yielding a value 0-127. This index is used to access palette[index] without any check that index < palSize. Since only palSize entries of the 128-element palette array are initialized, an attacker can craft a malicious stream with an index >= palSize to read uninitialized stack memory. This could leak sensitive information or cause unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in RLE run length accumulation
vulnerabilities.long_description: In both the plain RLE and palette RLE branches, the run length is accumulated by repeatedly adding byte values (up to 255) to an int variable `len`. A malicious server can send a long sequence of 255 bytes, eventually causing `len` to overflow the 32-bit signed int. Once overflowed, `len` could become negative, bypassing the `end - ptr < len` bounds check (since the comparison with a negative len would pass), potentially leading to a heap or stack buffer underflow/overflow when writing pixels to buf.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.short_description: strncpy may not null-terminate sun_path, leading to buffer over-read or undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: strncpy does not null-terminate the destination if the source string length is >= sizeof(local.sun_path). If pipePath is longer than or equal to the size of sun_path (typically 108 bytes), local.sun_path will not be null-terminated. Subsequent use of local.sun_path (e.g., in unlink() and chmod()) treats it as a C string, causing reads past the buffer boundary. An attacker who controls the endpoint name could craft a path of exactly the right length to trigger this, potentially unlinking or chmod-ing unintended files, or causing crashes.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of listen() is not checked for errors
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of ::listen() is not checked. If listen() fails, the code continues as though the socket is ready to accept connections, adding the file descriptor to pipeServerSockets. This could lead to unexpected behavior when clients try to connect later. While not directly exploitable, it violates secure coding practices and could leave the server in an inconsistent state.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds check on i_lenAlloc against PAGE_SIZE before page memset/memcpy
vulnerabilities.long_description: If iinfo->i_lenAlloc is larger than PAGE_SIZE (e.g., from a corrupted on-disk UDF filesystem image), the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` will produce an integer underflow (unsigned wraparound), causing memset to write a huge amount of zeroes past the mapped page. The subsequent memcpy would also write past the page boundary. An attacker who crafts a malicious UDF image could trigger an out-of-bounds kernel memory write, leading to privilege escalation or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Error-path recovery uses inode->i_size as copy length without validating it fits in i_data buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path after filemap_fdatawrite fails, the code copies `inode->i_size` bytes from the page back into the inline inode data area at offset `i_lenEAttr`. There is no check that `i_lenEAttr + i_size` fits within the allocated size of the `i_data` buffer. If `inode->i_size` is larger than the space available (for instance due to a crafted filesystem or a race condition), this will overflow the `i_data` buffer in the inode info structure, corrupting adjacent kernel heap memory. This could be exploited for privilege escalation.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if parse_attr_value returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of parse_attr_value is assigned to buf but never checked for NULL before being used in the next loop iteration. If parse_attr_value fails and returns NULL, the next call will pass NULL as the buffer pointer and compute `buf_end - buf` which is undefined behavior on a NULL pointer, leading to a crash or worse.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < abbrev->count - 1; i++) {
			memset(&die->attr_values[i], 0, sizeof(die->attr_values[i]));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on die->attr_values if its allocated size is smaller than abbrev->count - 1
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterates up to abbrev->count - 1 times and indexes into die->attr_values[i] without any bounds check against the actual allocated capacity of die->attr_values. If abbrev->count is larger than the allocated array size, this results in a heap buffer overflow (both read and write) via the memset, parse_attr_value write, and subsequent attribute reads. An attacker crafting a malicious DWARF binary could set a large count in the abbreviation table to trigger this overflow, potentially achieving code execution.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < abbrev->count - 1; i++) {
		memset(&die->attr_values[i], 0, sizeof(die->attr_values[i]));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when abbrev->count is 0 causes massive out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: If abbrev->count is 0, the expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows (since count is likely an unsigned type such as size_t), resulting in a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF for 64-bit size_t). This causes the loop to iterate far beyond the bounds of die->attr_values and abbrev->defs arrays, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes via memset, parse_attr_value, and subsequent accesses. An attacker crafting a malicious DWARF binary with count=0 could exploit this for information disclosure or code execution.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
			&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on buf returned from parse_attr_value
vulnerabilities.long_description: If parse_attr_value returns NULL (e.g., on a parse error or truncated input), the returned NULL pointer is passed back into parse_attr_value on the next iteration without any check. This leads to a NULL pointer dereference or, depending on the implementation, reads from near-zero memory addresses. A malformed DWARF input could trigger this condition.

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of i_lenAlloc against PAGE_SIZE can cause out-of-bounds write via integer underflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: If iinfo->i_lenAlloc is greater than PAGE_SIZE (possible from a corrupted or malicious UDF filesystem image), the expression `PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc` wraps to a very large value due to unsigned integer arithmetic. This causes the memset to write far beyond the mapped page buffer. Additionally, the memcpy with a large i_lenAlloc would read out-of-bounds from i_data. An attacker crafting a malicious UDF image with an inflated i_lenAlloc value for an in-ICB file could trigger kernel heap/stack corruption, potentially leading to denial of service or privilege escalation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Error-recovery path copies inode->i_size bytes into i_data without bounds checking, risking buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the error recovery path after filemap_fdatawrite fails, the code copies inode->i_size bytes from the page back into iinfo->i_data. The i_data buffer has a fixed size, and if inode->i_size exceeds the available space (i.e., the size of i_data minus i_lenEAttr), this memcpy will overflow the i_data buffer. A crafted or corrupted filesystem where i_size has been manipulated could trigger an out-of-bounds write into kernel memory, potentially leading to privilege escalation or system compromise.

commit_message: Fixed bug #72479 - same as #72434
cve_desc: ext/snmp/snmp.c in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9 improperly interacts with the unserialize implementation and garbage collection, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (use-after-free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via crafted serialized data, a related issue to CVE-2016-5773.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in heap allocation when reading .gpb brush+pattern combination
vulnerabilities.long_description: When bh.bytes is 1 and a valid pattern header follows (the obsolete .gpb format), the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes. However, the initial sanitization only checked that `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with bh.bytes=1, meaning width*height can be up to G_MAXSIZE. The multiplication `4 * bh.width * bh.height` can then overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. The subsequent loop writes `4 * width * height` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious .gpb file with large width/height values to trigger this overflow, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential signed/unsigned comparison issue when read() return value is compared with gsize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `size` is of type `gsize` (unsigned), but `read()` returns `ssize_t` (signed), which has a maximum value of SSIZE_MAX. If `size` exceeds SSIZE_MAX, the read() call has undefined behavior per POSIX, and the comparison `read(fd, brush_buf, size) != size` may not detect a short read or error correctly. The sanitization check allows `width * height * bytes` up to G_MAXSIZE, which can exceed SSIZE_MAX on platforms where size_t is the same width as ssize_t. This could lead to processing uninitialized heap memory as image data.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when bin_obj is NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function checks that bf and bf->o are non-null, but does not check that bf->o->bin_obj (qo) is non-null before dereferencing it to access qo->fixups. If bin_obj is NULL, this leads to a null pointer dereference, which can cause a crash (denial of service) when processing a malformed binary file.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read via strchr on non-null-terminated stack buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function copies `nbytes` bytes from `buf` into the stack-allocated `drc_name` using `memcpy`, which does not null-terminate the destination buffer. It then calls `strchr(drc_name, '\n')`, which scans memory byte-by-byte looking for a newline or implicitly stopping at a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is not null-terminated and may contain uninitialized stack data beyond the copied region, `strchr` can read past the `nbytes` copied bytes into uninitialized stack memory. If no '\n' or '\0' is found within the `MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN`-sized buffer, it could even read beyond the buffer entirely. While the NULL return from `strchr` is handled, the out-of-bounds read itself is undefined behavior and could potentially leak sensitive stack information or cause other issues depending on the platform.

Problem:    Using freed memory with cmdwin and BufEnter autocmd.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can overflow if `data_len` is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX). This would result in a small value for `outlen`, causing `emalloc` to allocate a small buffer. The subsequent `EVP_DecryptUpdate` call would then write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker could supply a large raw input (or a large base64-encoded input) to trigger this condition, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when base64 decoding fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `php_base64_decode` returns NULL (e.g., due to invalid input or memory allocation failure), `data` is set to NULL without any check. This NULL pointer is then passed to `EVP_DecryptUpdate`, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash. An attacker could supply malformed non-raw input data to trigger this condition, resulting in a denial of service.

patch 9.0.0490: using freed memory with cmdwin and BufEnter autocmd

Solution:   Make sure pointer to b_p_iminsert is still valid.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in output buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `data_len` is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), the addition of `EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` (and the subsequent `+1` in emalloc) can cause an integer overflow, wrapping around to a small value. This results in a tiny heap allocation, while `EVP_EncryptUpdate` then writes up to the original `data_len + block_size` bytes into that undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who controls the length of the `data` parameter can exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing error-checking on EVP_EncryptInit, EVP_CIPHER_CTX_set_key_length, and EVP_EncryptUpdate return values
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return values of EVP_EncryptInit, EVP_CIPHER_CTX_set_key_length, EVP_EncryptInit_ex, and EVP_EncryptUpdate are never checked. If any of these calls fail (e.g., set_key_length fails for a fixed-length cipher), execution continues with an improperly initialized cipher context. This can lead to use of uninitialized memory, incorrect encryption output, or other undefined behavior that could compromise data confidentiality.

commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when reading BMP V4/V5 headers (biSize 108 or 124)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'buffer' array is declared as 64 bytes, but when Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5), the code reads biSize - 4 bytes (104 or 120 bytes) into this 64-byte buffer. This causes a stack-based buffer overflow, which can be exploited by a crafted BMP file to overwrite the return address or other stack data, leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer passed to fclose when fopen fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If fopen() fails and returns NULL, the code jumps to the 'cleanup' label which calls fclose(fd) with a NULL pointer. Passing NULL to fclose() is undefined behavior per the C standard and will likely cause a crash (segmentation fault). The cleanup block should check if fd is non-NULL before calling fclose.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting width/height to unsigned short
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers but are cast to unsigned short (16-bit) when initializing the bitmap. If the image dimensions exceed 65535, the values will be silently truncated, causing a mismatch between the allocated image data (based on full 32-bit dimensions) and the bitmap metadata (truncated 16-bit dimensions). This can lead to subsequent out-of-bounds memory access when the image is processed.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to wrong index variable in duplicate namespace check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The inner loop uses index 'j' to access both nspace_declarations[j] and element->attributes[j], but it should use 'i' for element->attributes (the current outer-loop attribute). When nspace_declarations_count exceeds element->attribute_count, element->attributes[j] reads past the end of the attributes array, leading to an out-of-bounds read. An attacker crafting XML with many declared namespaces could trigger this, potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
    unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when escaping xml:lang value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer is allocated with size buf_length+1 (i.e., 10 + lang_len + 2). The pointer 'p' is set 10 bytes (XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN) into the buffer, so only lang_len+2 bytes remain. However, raptor_xml_escape_string is passed 'buf_length' (10 + lang_len + 1) as the output buffer size, allowing it to write far past the actual remaining space. Since XML escaping expands characters (e.g., '&' → '&'), the escaped output can easily exceed the allocated buffer. An attacker providing a specially crafted xml:lang string with many escapable characters could overflow the heap buffer, potentially achieving code execution.

code_snippets: unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if malloc fails for xml:lang buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of RAPTOR_MALLOC for the xml:lang buffer is not checked for NULL before being used in memcpy. If the allocation fails, a NULL pointer dereference occurs, causing a crash. This could be triggered under memory pressure or with a very large xml:lang value.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read/write via unbounded depArray index and unchecked dep_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterating over depArray uses a sentinel value (0xffff) as the termination condition but never checks that 'k' stays within the allocated bounds of depArray (cache->accel->depListCount elements). If the data is malformed and the sentinel is missing, this reads past the buffer. Additionally, 'dep_index' (depArray[k] & 0x7fff) is used to index both 'deps' (sized cache->hdr->imagesCount) and 'img' (sized hdr->imagesCount) without any bounds check, allowing an attacker-controlled cache file to cause out-of-bounds reads and writes.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
	bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on deps array when subsequent headers have larger imagesCount
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'deps' array is allocated based on cache->hdr->imagesCount (i.e., the first header's image count). However, the outer loop iterates over all headers (cache->n_hdr), and the inner loop iterates up to each header's own imagesCount. If any subsequent header has a larger imagesCount than the first, accesses to deps[j] will be out of bounds, leading to heap buffer overflow (both read and write). A crafted dyld cache with a smaller first header imagesCount and larger subsequent ones would trigger this.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when allocating RGBA brush buffer in the .gpb pattern-following code path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When bh.bytes is 1 and a valid pattern follows (the .gpb path), the code allocates a new buffer with `g_malloc(4 * bh.width * bh.height)`. The original overflow check `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` was performed with bh.bytes=1, so it only validates that `bh.width * bh.height` fits in gsize. However, multiplying by 4 can overflow gsize (especially on 32-bit systems), resulting in a small heap allocation. The subsequent loop then writes `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow that could allow arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Brush name buffer passed as null-terminated string without guaranteed null terminator
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `temp` buffer of size `bn_size` is read from the file and then passed to `gimp_any_to_utf8` with a length of -1, which means the function treats it as a null-terminated C string. However, the file data is not guaranteed to contain a null terminator within the `bn_size` bytes. This can cause the function to read beyond the allocated heap buffer until it finds a null byte, leading to an out-of-bounds heap read. An attacker can craft a brush file with a name field that lacks a null terminator to potentially leak heap memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak when header validation fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the header sanitization check fails (invalid width, height, bytes, or overflow condition), the function returns -1 without calling `close(fd)`. This leaks the open file descriptor. While primarily a resource leak, in long-running applications or when processing many attacker-controlled files, this can lead to file descriptor exhaustion (denial of service).

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow in stack_rev loop leads to out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). When `stack_rev` is true, the loop decrements `i` and checks `i >= to`. Since `to` can be 0 (when `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0 or `fcn->cc` is NULL), the condition `i >= 0` is always true for an unsigned type. Once `i` reaches 0 and is decremented, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX, and the loop continues indefinitely, causing out-of-bounds reads via `r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, i)` and `r_type_func_args_name(anal->sdb_types, fname, i)` with enormous index values. An attacker who can influence function type information or calling convention metadata could trigger this to cause a denial of service (infinite loop) or potentially leak/corrupt memory.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to image->colormap when image->colors exceeds MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the OpenMP parallel loop, image->colors is incremented inside the critical section each time a new intensity value is encountered, but there is no bounds check to ensure image->colors does not exceed MaxColormapSize (the size of the allocated colormap). If the image contains more than MaxColormapSize distinct intensity values, this results in an out-of-bounds write on image->colormap, leading to heap buffer overflow. An attacker could craft an image to trigger this and achieve code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on colormap_index when image is PseudoClass
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the image storage class is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with only image->colors+1 entries. However, in the final parallel loop, it is indexed by ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q)), which can return values up to MaxColormapSize-1, far exceeding the allocated size. Similarly, colormap_index[(ssize_t) image->colormap[i].alpha] = j uses the alpha field (set to the original index i), which could also exceed the allocated bounds if image->colors changed. This leads to heap-based out-of-bounds read/write, potentially exploitable for information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition in double-checked locking on colormap_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code uses a double-checked locking pattern: it first reads colormap_index[intensity] outside the critical section, then rechecks inside it. Without memory barriers or atomic operations, the first read of colormap_index[intensity] is a data race—another thread inside the critical section may be writing to colormap_index[intensity] concurrently. On architectures with weak memory ordering, the outer check could see a stale value, and image->colors could be read/written non-atomically across threads, leading to duplicate colormap entries or corrupted state.
