commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Option payload not drained from socket before continuing, causing protocol desynchronization
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the early-rejection checks fire (non-fixed-newstyle client sending an option other than EXPORT_NAME, or a non-TLS client in FORCEDTLS mode), the code sends an error reply and 'continue's to the next loop iteration without reading the 'optlen' bytes of option payload from the socket. On the next iteration, 'conn_recv_full(&new_option, sizeof new_option, ...)' will read leftover payload bytes as the next option header. An attacker can craft the payload of a rejected option so that these leftover bytes are interpreted as a new, valid option header (e.g. NBD_OPT_EXPORT_NAME), potentially bypassing security checks such as the TLS requirement. In the --tls=require case, this could allow an unauthenticated client to reach the export-name handling code and gain access to the backend without establishing a TLS session.

commit_message: Bug: Lua can generate wrong code when _ENV is <const>
cve_desc: singlevar in lparser.c in Lua from (including) 5.4.0 up to (excluding) 5.4.4 lacks a certain luaK_exp2anyregup call, leading to a heap-based buffer over-read that might affect a system that compiles untrusted Lua code.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4218: illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode

Problem:    Illegal memory access with bracketed paste in Ex mode.
Solution:   Reserve space for the trailing NUL.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow when accumulating characters in buf without bounds checking on idx
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop accumulates characters into the stack buffer `buf` of size `NUMBUFLEN + MB_MAXBYTES`. Each iteration adds up to MB_MAXBYTES bytes (for multibyte) or 1 byte to `buf[idx]`, but `idx` is never checked against the buffer size before writing. If the end-of-paste termcode is not found (e.g., `end` is NULL, or the paste stream is long without matching the end sequence), `idx` grows unbounded, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. The only reset of `idx` to 0 happens after the accumulated buffer is consumed (when it doesn't prefix-match `end`), but while prefix-matching is in progress, characters keep accumulating. If an attacker crafts input that repeatedly partially matches the end code, `idx` can exceed `NUMBUFLEN + MB_MAXBYTES`, corrupting the stack and potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
seq_file: disallow extremely large seq buffer allocations

There is no reasonable need for a buffer larger than this, and it avoids
int overflow pitfalls.

Fixes: 058504edd026 ("fs/seq_file: fallback to vmalloc allocation")
Suggested-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Reported-by: Qualys Security Advisory <qsa@qualys.com>
Signed-off-by: Eric Sandeen <sandeen@redhat.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: fs/seq_file.c in the Linux kernel 3.16 through 5.13.x before 5.13.4 does not properly restrict seq buffer allocations, leading to an integer overflow, an Out-of-bounds Write, and escalation to root by an unprivileged user, aka CID-8cae8cd89f05.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>

cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing NULL check on alloc_workqueue return value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of alloc_workqueue() is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, trans_pcie->rba.alloc_wq will be NULL, and subsequent use of this workqueue (e.g., queue_work) will result in a NULL pointer dereference, causing a kernel crash/panic.

cve_desc: In Artifex MuJS through 1.2.0, jsP_dumpsyntax in jsdump.c has a NULL pointer dereference, as demonstrated by mujs-pp.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read/write via unbounded depArray traversal and unchecked dep_index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop iterating over depArray uses a sentinel value (0xffff) as the termination condition but never checks that the index 'k' stays within the allocated bounds of depArray (cache->accel->depListCount). If the sentinel is missing or the start index from extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex is corrupted, the loop reads past the buffer. Additionally, 'dep_index' (depArray[k] & 0x7fff) is used to index both 'deps' (sized cache->hdr->imagesCount, i.e. the first header's count) and 'img' (sized for the current header's imagesCount) without any bounds validation. A crafted dyld cache file can cause out-of-bounds reads and writes, leading to information disclosure or arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
	bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on deps array when a sub-header has more images than the first header
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'deps' array is allocated using cache->hdr->imagesCount (the image count from the first cache header, i.e. cache->hdr[0]). However, the inner loop iterates up to hdr->imagesCount for each sub-header (cache->hdr[i]), which may be larger than cache->hdr[0].imagesCount. When i > 0, accessing deps[j] where j >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount results in a heap buffer overflow (both read and write). An attacker can craft a cache with a later header having a larger imagesCount to trigger this.

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN characters. However, on some filesystems, `dp->d_name` (from `readdir()`) can exceed BASENAMELEN. The unchecked `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies the directory entry name into `buf` without verifying its length fits within the allocation. Additionally, in the starstar branch, "/**" (3 bytes) plus `path_end` are appended after the entry name, further increasing the required space. An attacker who can create files with names longer than BASENAMELEN on a supporting filesystem (or via a malicious filesystem like FUSE) could trigger a heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when NUL-terminating the input buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code writes a NUL byte at `tp[len]` to terminate the string. When a caller-provided `buf` is used, `tp = buf + offset` and `len = *buflen - offset`, so the write goes to `buf[*buflen]`. If `*buflen` equals `bufsize` (the allocated size of `buf`), this writes one byte past the end of the buffer. Similarly, when using `typebuf.tb_buf`, the write is at `typebuf.tb_buf[typebuf.tb_off + typebuf.tb_len]`, which may exceed the allocated buffer size. An attacker who can influence the terminal input buffer contents and length could trigger heap corruption, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: mixer is deleted then dereferenced
vulnerabilities.long_description: The destructor deletes `mixer` first, then in the `if (player)` block it dereferences `mixer` by calling `mixer->isActive()` and `mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player)`. Since `mixer` was already deleted, this is a use-after-free. If `mixer` was non-null and `player` is also non-null, the code accesses freed memory, leading to undefined behavior which could result in crashes or potentially exploitable memory corruption.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds array access due to wrong index variable in duplicate namespace check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The inner loop uses 'j' to index both nspace_declarations and element->attributes, but it should use 'i' (the current outer-loop attribute index) for element->attributes. Since nspace_declarations_count can grow larger than element->attribute_count (it includes element namespace, declared namespaces, and attribute values), the index j can exceed the bounds of element->attributes, causing an out-of-bounds read. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash, and potentially exploitable memory corruption if the read pointer is dereferenced further.

code_snippets: unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when XML-escaping xml:lang value
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer is allocated with size based on the raw length of xml_language (lang_len), but XML escaping can significantly expand the string (e.g., '&' -> '&', '"' -> '"'). The allocated buffer only has lang_len+2 bytes available after the 'xml:lang="' prefix, which is insufficient for escaped output. Furthermore, the 'buf_length' parameter passed to raptor_xml_escape_string is XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1, which is larger than the actual remaining space in the buffer (lang_len + 2 bytes from pointer p), causing the escape function to believe more space is available than actually exists. A crafted xml_language string with many escapable characters could overflow the heap buffer.

code_snippets: unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if malloc fails for xml:lang buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of RAPTOR_MALLOC for the xml:lang buffer is not checked for NULL before being used in memcpy. If the allocation fails, a NULL pointer will be passed to memcpy, resulting in undefined behavior and likely a crash. Other allocations in this function are properly checked, but this one is missing the NULL check and corresponding goto error.

commit_message:

Commit f1b0890 introduced a potential crash due to incorrect operator

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds read on pointer array
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` due to `[]` having higher precedence than unary `*`. The intended expression is `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When `i > 0`, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory beyond the single `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` pointer parameter, treating the caller's stack or heap memory as a pointer-to-pointer, then dereferencing it. This results in out-of-bounds reads and potentially passing garbage pointers to `virMediatedDeviceTypeFree`, leading to crashes or memory corruption. An attacker who can trigger a device refresh with existing mdev types could exploit this.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential NULL pointer dereference if platform_get_resource for MEM returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of the second `platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0)` call is not checked for NULL before being passed to `devm_ioremap_resource`. While `devm_ioremap_resource` internally handles a NULL resource by returning an ERR_PTR, the subsequent accesses to `res->start` and `res->end` after the `IS_ERR` check could theoretically be reached if `devm_ioremap_resource`'s internal behavior changes or on certain kernel versions where it does not check for NULL. In current mainline kernels, `devm_ioremap_resource` does return an error for NULL, making this safe in practice, but the missing explicit NULL check is a defensive coding deficiency that has been flagged in similar kernel code. If `res` were NULL and the error path were somehow not taken, a kernel NULL pointer dereference (kernel oops/panic) would result.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when directory entry name exceeds BASENAMELEN
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names are at most BASENAMELEN bytes long. However, `readdir()` can return entries with names longer than BASENAMELEN on certain filesystems (e.g., some network or FUSE filesystems where NAME_MAX differs). The `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` call copies the directory entry name into `buf` at offset `s` without any length check. Subsequently, `STRCPY(buf + len, "/**")` and `STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end)` append additional data. If `dp->d_name` is longer than BASENAMELEN, this overflows the heap buffer, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker who can control filenames on a filesystem being glob-expanded (e.g., by creating specially named files) could exploit this.

commit_message: Fix crash when mapping a big memory and calling uc_close
cve_desc: Unicorn Engine v2.0.0-rc7 and below was discovered to contain a NULL pointer dereference via qemu_ram_free.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT is sent multiple times without freeing previous allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the client sends NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT multiple times, conn->exportname_from_set_meta_context is overwritten with a new strndup allocation without first freeing the previous value. While the loop is bounded by MAX_NR_OPTIONS, a malicious client could repeatedly send this option with large export names (up to MAX_REQUEST_SIZE) to cause memory accumulation. Compare this with the NBD_OPT_STARTTLS handler which correctly calls free(conn->exportname_from_set_meta_context) before setting it to NULL.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when platform_get_resource for IORESOURCE_MEM returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of `platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0)` is not checked for NULL before being passed to `devm_ioremap_resource`. While some kernel versions of `devm_ioremap_resource` handle a NULL `res` internally, in older kernels it does not, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. Even if `devm_ioremap_resource` handles NULL gracefully, the subsequent accesses to `res->start` and `res->end` after the `IS_ERR` check on `lp->base_addr` are only safe if `devm_ioremap_resource` returned an error for a NULL `res` — but this is an implicit and fragile assumption. If the platform resource is unavailable, this can cause a kernel oops/panic.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>

cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation for surfaceData allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic, which can overflow if width and height are sufficiently large (e.g., width=65536, height=65536 would wrap to 0 or a small value). This could result in allocating a much smaller buffer than intended, leading to subsequent heap buffer overflows when pixel data is written into `surfaceData`. An attacker who controls the presentation dimensions (e.g., via a malicious server) could exploit this to achieve remote code execution.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write on image->colormap when image->colors exceeds MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The colormap is allocated with MaxColormapSize entries via AcquireImageColormap, but image->colors is incremented without any bounds check against MaxColormapSize. If the number of unique grayscale intensities exceeds MaxColormapSize (e.g., for higher quantum depths where ScaleQuantumToMap can produce more than 256 unique values), writes to image->colormap[image->colors] will go out of bounds, leading to heap buffer overflow. This could be exploited by crafting an image with many distinct grayscale values to corrupt heap memory, potentially achieving code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized colormap_index entries when MaxMap exceeds MaxColormapSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The colormap_index array is allocated with MagickMax(MaxColormapSize+1, MaxMap) elements, but memset only initializes MaxColormapSize elements to -1. For quantum depths where MaxMap > MaxColormapSize (e.g., 16-bit quantum where MaxMap=65535 and MaxColormapSize=256), entries from index MaxColormapSize through MaxMap-1 remain uninitialized. Since ScaleQuantumToMap can return values up to MaxMap, the check `colormap_index[intensity] < 0` reads uninitialized memory. If the uninitialized value happens to be non-negative, it will be used as an index into image->colormap, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads/writes and unpredictable behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition: colormap_index[intensity] read outside critical section while written inside
vulnerabilities.long_description: The first check `if (colormap_index[intensity] < 0)` is performed outside the critical section, while the value is written inside the critical section. Although there is a double-checked locking pattern, the outer read of colormap_index[intensity] is not atomic and can race with the write inside the critical section from another thread. Additionally, `image->colors` is read/written by `SetPixelIndex` usage and `image->colormap` accesses outside the critical section, creating potential data races. On architectures without strong memory ordering, this could lead to inconsistent state and incorrect colormap indices.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Pipe buffer flags not initialized when creating a new pipe buffer entry (CVE-2022-0847 / Dirty Pipe)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a new pipe buffer is populated, the code sets `ops`, `page`, `offset`, and `len`, but it never clears or initializes `buf->flags`. The `flags` field may retain stale values from a previous use of the ring buffer slot, most critically the `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` flag. If this flag is left set, a subsequent `pipe_write()` call will treat the buffer as mergeable and write directly into the referenced page cache page rather than allocating a private copy. This allows an unprivileged user to overwrite arbitrary page cache contents (including read-only files, SUID binaries, etc.) leading to local privilege escalation. The fix is to add `buf->flags = 0;` when initializing the new buffer entry.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen leads to undersized heap allocation and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t` (max value 65535), but the sum of `strlen(provider_name) + strlen(ttl_class_type) + 4*sizeof(struct SignedCert) + 2` can easily exceed 65535. The guard clause only checks if `strlen(provider_name) >= 0xffff0000` (~4 billion), which is far too large to protect a 16-bit variable from wrapping. If the provider name is long enough that the total exceeds 65535 but is under ~4 billion, `rrlen` silently wraps around to a small value, causing `malloc` to allocate a tiny buffer. Subsequent `snprintf` calls then write far beyond the allocated buffer, resulting in a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who controls or influences the provider name in the configuration could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Return value of strdup() not checked for NULL before use
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple calls to `strdup()` are made without checking for NULL return values. If memory allocation fails, NULL pointers are passed directly to `cfg_str2list_insert` and `cfg_strlist_insert`, which may dereference them, leading to a NULL pointer dereference crash (denial of service).

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.short_description: fclose called on NULL file pointer when fopen fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When fopen() fails, fd is NULL and execution jumps to the cleanup label which unconditionally calls fclose(fd). Passing NULL to fclose() is undefined behavior and will likely cause a crash (NULL pointer dereference). This can be triggered by providing a non-existent or inaccessible filename.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation of image dimensions when casting to unsigned short
vulnerabilities.long_description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight (32-bit integers) are cast to unsigned short (16-bit). If the BMP specifies dimensions larger than 65535, the values silently truncate. This creates a mismatch between the actual image data size (allocated by ReadImage using the full 32-bit dimensions) and the dimensions recorded in the at_bitmap structure, potentially leading to heap buffer over-reads or other memory corruption when the bitmap is later processed.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in rowbytes calculation despite earlier overflow check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The overflow check uses unsigned long for overflowTest, but the rowbytes calculation uses int arithmetic (both biWidth and biBitCnt could be int). If the multiplication overflows the int type before being used in the rowbytes formula, the computed rowbytes value will be incorrect (potentially too small), causing ReadImage to read incorrect amounts of data, leading to heap buffer overflows or other memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in ColormapSize calculation with attacker-controlled bfOffs
vulnerabilities.long_description: If a malicious BMP file has bfOffs set to a value smaller than (biSize + 14), the subtraction underflows. If the types are unsigned, this results in a very large positive value. Although ColormapSize is later capped at 256, the intermediate large value divided by Maps (3 or 4) could still exceed 256 in some cases or cause unexpected behavior. If the types are signed, the result is negative, and dividing a negative by Maps yields a negative ColormapSize, which could bypass the > 256 check and cause issues in ReadColorMap.

commit_message:
io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
Reported-and-tested-by: syzbot+9671693590ef5aad8953@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Pavel Begunkov <asml.silence@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to missing bounds check on 'depth' before indexing MAXWLEN-sized arrays
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'depth' is incremented and used to index stack-allocated arrays (arridx, curi, tword, wordcount) of size MAXWLEN without any upper-bound check. If the trie being traversed contains a path longer than MAXWLEN bytes, 'depth' will exceed the array bounds, causing out-of-bounds writes on the stack. An attacker who can craft a malicious spell file with a sufficiently deep trie can trigger this overflow to overwrite the return address or other stack data, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing tok->args[2] after only checking tok->n_args > 1
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks `tok->n_args > 1` (meaning n_args >= 2), then parses `tok->args[1]`. However, in the error-handling path, it logs `tok->args[2]` instead of `tok->args[1]`. When `tok->n_args` is exactly 2, `tok->args[2]` is an out-of-bounds access. An attacker can craft a malicious network status document with a `network-status-version` line containing exactly two arguments (e.g., `network-status-version 3 bogus_flavor`) where the flavor name is unrecognized, triggering the out-of-bounds read in the log statement. This could lead to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stack buffer overflow due to unchecked depth increment when traversing the trie
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `depth` is incremented without any bounds check against MAXWLEN before being used to index into the stack-allocated arrays `arridx`, `curi`, `tword`, and `wordcount` (all of size MAXWLEN). If the trie contains a path longer than MAXWLEN characters, the depth will exceed the array bounds, leading to a stack-based buffer overflow. An attacker who can control the contents of the spell file (and thus the trie structure) could craft a malicious spell file with an excessively deep trie path, overwriting adjacent stack memory, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220311080208.45047-1-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marc Kleine-Budde <mkl@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: mcba_usb_start_xmit in drivers/net/can/usb/mcba_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.17.1 has a double free.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to missing bounds check on s->reg index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads from s->edid_blob using s->reg as an index without any bounds checking. If s->reg exceeds the size of the edid_blob array (e.g., through repeated calls to this function), the read will go out of bounds, potentially leaking adjacent memory contents. An attacker interacting with the emulated I2C DDC device could repeatedly read to increment s->reg past the buffer boundary, causing an information disclosure or crash.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4151: reading beyond the end of a line

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of a line.
Solution:   For block insert only use the offset for correcting the length.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Truncation of size_t to unsigned int can cause undersized buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable s_len is declared as 'unsigned' (typically 32 bits) but is assigned the result of STRLEN(), which returns size_t (64 bits on 64-bit platforms). If the string 's' is longer than UINT_MAX, the length is silently truncated. This truncated value is then used in the alloc() size calculation, potentially resulting in a heap buffer that is far smaller than needed. While the subsequent mch_memmove also uses the truncated s_len (limiting the direct copy), the overall buffer arithmetic—including the STRMOVE of the old line tail—relies on an offset computed from the truncated s_len, which could place writes beyond the allocated buffer or cause other memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size computation on 32-bit systems
vulnerabilities.long_description: The allocation size is computed by summing STRLEN(oldp) (size_t), spaces (int), s_len (unsigned), ts_val-related terms, and count (int). On 32-bit systems where size_t is 32 bits, if oldp is a very long line and s_len is large, this addition can wrap around, producing a small allocation. Subsequent memcpy/memmove and STRMOVE operations would then write well beyond the allocated heap buffer, leading to heap buffer overflow, which can be exploited for arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Incorrect size calculation for the post-match substring in backward search leads to out-of-bounds read.
vulnerabilities.long_description: When searching backward (`forward` is false), the size assigned to the post-match substring (`op1`) is calculated as `count + size - 1`. At the point a match is found at position `j` (offset from the start of the haystack), the variable `count` equals `j`. The correct remaining size after the match is `(original_count) - j` (i.e., `r_size(op1) - size - j`), but the code computes `j + size - 1` instead. When the match occurs near the end of the haystack (large `j`), this computed size can be significantly larger than the actual remaining bytes, causing the returned string reference to extend far beyond the original string buffer. Any subsequent read of that string results in an out-of-bounds memory read (heap buffer over-read). For example, if the haystack is length 10, the pattern is length 3, and the match is found at offset 7 (the last possible position), `count = 7` and the code sets the post-match size to `7 + 3 - 1 = 9`, but the actual remaining data is 0 bytes. An attacker could exploit this to leak sensitive heap memory contents.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen can lead to undersized heap allocation and buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `rrlen` is declared as `uint16_t`, which has a maximum value of 65535. The length calculation sums `strlen(dnscenv->provider_name)`, a fixed string length, and `4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert)`. If `provider_name` is sufficiently long, the sum wraps around the 16-bit range, resulting in a very small value for `rrlen`. This causes `malloc(rrlen)` to allocate a tiny buffer, while subsequent `snprintf` calls write the full provider name and certificate data into it, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. An attacker who controls the provider_name configuration could exploit this to achieve arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked strdup return values may pass NULL pointers to functions that dereference them
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple calls to `strdup()` are made without checking their return values for NULL. If memory allocation fails, NULL is passed to `cfg_str2list_insert` or `cfg_strlist_insert`, which likely dereference the pointer, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. This constitutes a denial-of-service vulnerability under memory pressure conditions.

commit_message:
hamradio: improve the incomplete fix to avoid NPD

The previous commit 3e0588c291d6 ("hamradio: defer ax25 kfree after
unregister_netdev") reorder the kfree operations and unregister_netdev
operation to prevent UAF.

This commit improves the previous one by also deferring the nullify of
the ax->tty pointer. Otherwise, a NULL pointer dereference bug occurs.
Partial of the stack trace is shown below.

BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000538
RIP: 0010:ax_xmit+0x1f9/0x400
...
Call Trace:
 dev_hard_start_xmit+0xec/0x320
 sch_direct_xmit+0xea/0x240
 __qdisc_run+0x166/0x5c0
 __dev_queue_xmit+0x2c7/0xaf0
 ax25_std_establish_data_link+0x59/0x60
 ax25_connect+0x3a0/0x500
 ? security_socket_connect+0x2b/0x40
 __sys_connect+0x96/0xc0
 ? __hrtimer_init+0xc0/0xc0
 ? common_nsleep+0x2e/0x50
 ? switch_fpu_return+0x139/0x1a0
 __x64_sys_connect+0x11/0x20
 do_syscall_64+0x33/0x40
 entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xa9

The crash point is shown as below

static void ax_encaps(...) {
  ...
  set_bit(TTY_DO_WRITE_WAKEUP, &ax->tty->flags); // ax->tty = NULL!
  ...
}

By placing the nullify action after the unregister_netdev, the ax->tty
pointer won't be assigned as NULL net_device framework layer is well
synchronized.

Signed-off-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel in drivers/net/hamradio. This flaw allows a local attacker with a user privilege to cause a denial of service (DOS) when the mkiss or sixpack device is detached and reclaim resources early.

The crash point is shown as below

static void ax_encaps(...) {
  ...
  set_bit(TTY_DO_WRITE_WAKEUP, &ax->tty->flags); // ax->tty = NULL!
  ...
}

By placing the nullify action after the unregister_netdev, the ax->tty
pointer won't be assigned as NULL net_device framework layer is well
synchronized.

Signed-off-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel in drivers/net/hamradio. This flaw allows a local attacker with a user privilege to cause a denial of service (DOS) when the mkiss or sixpack device is detached and reclaim resources early.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in surface buffer size calculation due to 32-bit intermediate multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `width * height * 4ULL` evaluates `width * height` first using 32-bit unsigned arithmetic (both are UINT32), which can wrap around before the result is promoted to 64-bit for the multiplication by 4ULL. For example, if width=65536 and height=65536, `width * height` overflows to 0 as a UINT32, then `0 * 4ULL = 0`, which passes the `INT32_MAX` check. This leads to `BufferPool_Take` allocating a zero-sized (or very small) buffer, while subsequent operations (h264 decoding, YUV conversion) write data for the full width*height surface, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker controlling the width and height parameters could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

commit_message: Fix oobread in symbols header parsing ##bin
cve_desc: Heap Buffer Overflow in parseDragons in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of buffer `b` on early return paths after allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `b` is allocated with malloc but is not freed when the function returns NULL after the r_buf_read_at calls fail. Both the first r_buf_read_at check (line with `available != size`) and the retry r_buf_read_at check inside the magicCombo block return NULL without calling free(b). This leads to a memory leak each time these error paths are taken. Additionally, the `file_name` parameter is marked R_OWN (caller transfers ownership) but is also leaked on these paths.

commit_message:
media: rc: prevent memory leak in cx23888_ir_probe

In cx23888_ir_probe if kfifo_alloc fails the allocated memory for state
should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Sean Young <sean@mess.org>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab+samsung@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when v4l2_device_register_subdev fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When v4l2_device_register_subdev() fails (ret != 0), the error path frees the kfifo but does not free the 'state' allocation itself, resulting in a memory leak. An attacker or repeated failure condition could cause kernel memory exhaustion over time.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using unchecked user-supplied num_queues
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues comes from the untrusted vhost-user message payload (ctx->msg.payload.inflight.num_queues) and is used directly as the loop bound to index dev->virtqueue[i] without being validated against dev->nr_vring (the actual number of allocated virtqueues). A malicious or buggy vhost-user frontend can supply a num_queues value larger than the allocated array, causing out-of-bounds reads/writes on the virtqueue pointer array, potentially leading to code execution or memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that num_queues * pervq_inflight_size fits within the mmap'd region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code maps a region of mmap_size bytes but then iterates num_queues times, advancing addr by pervq_inflight_size each iteration, and writes to the memory at that address. There is no check that (mmap_offset + num_queues * pervq_inflight_size) <= mmap_size. An attacker can craft a message where the inflight regions extend beyond the mapped memory, causing writes (e.g., setting desc_num) to go past the mmap'd region, resulting in a SIGSEGV or potential memory corruption if adjacent mappings exist.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow/truncation in buffer size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is declared as `int`, but `p - *arg` is a `ptrdiff_t` which can be larger than INT_MAX on 64-bit platforms. The cast `(int)(p - *arg + extra)` silently truncates the value. If the input string is sufficiently large (over ~2GB), `len` could wrap to a small or negative value. When passed to `alloc()` (which typically takes a `size_t`, zero-extending or reinterpreting the int), this results in allocating a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent copy loop then writes far beyond the allocated heap buffer, enabling heap corruption, code execution, or denial of service. Additionally, the `extra` variable is an `int` that is incremented by 5 for every `\<` escape sequence encountered; with a very large number of such sequences, `extra` itself could overflow before being added to the pointer difference, further corrupting the length calculation.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation leads to undersized allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables num_queues (uint16_t) and pervq_inflight_size (uint64_t) are derived from user-controlled message payload values. Their multiplication is stored in mmap_size (uint64_t). While a 16-bit times 64-bit overflow is unlikely to wrap uint64_t, the downstream allocation via inflight_mem_alloc could receive a very large value or, depending on get_pervq_shm_size_packed/split implementations, pervq_inflight_size itself could be excessively large or zero due to a crafted queue_size. If num_queues is 0, mmap_size becomes 0, and subsequent memset and loop iterations may behave unexpectedly. More critically, there is no validation that num_queues and queue_size are non-zero or within reasonable bounds before being used in arithmetic and memory operations.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < queue_size; j++)
				inflight_packed->desc[j].next = j + 1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing validation of num_queues and queue_size allows out-of-bounds writes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The num_queues and queue_size values come directly from the vhost-user message payload without any validation beyond the message size check. If queue_size is larger than what was actually accounted for by get_pervq_shm_size_packed(), the loop writing to inflight_packed->desc[j] could write beyond the allocated per-queue region into adjacent queue data or beyond the mmap'd region entirely. Similarly, if num_queues is zero, the mmap_size will be zero, yet the code proceeds to call memset on the allocation. An attacker controlling the vhost-user frontend can craft these values to cause heap/mmap corruption or denial of service.

commit_message:
media: rc: prevent memory leak in cx23888_ir_probe

In cx23888_ir_probe if kfifo_alloc fails the allocated memory for state
should be released.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when kfifo_alloc fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If kfifo_alloc fails, the function returns -ENOMEM without freeing the previously allocated 'state' object, resulting in a memory leak.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'state' when v4l2_device_register_subdev fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If v4l2_device_register_subdev fails, the error path frees the kfifo but does not free the 'state' object, resulting in a memory leak.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on dev->virtqueue[] using unchecked guest-supplied num_queues
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable num_queues is read directly from the guest-supplied message (ctx->msg.payload.inflight.num_queues) and used as the loop bound when indexing dev->virtqueue[i], without any validation that num_queues <= dev->nr_vring. A malicious guest/frontend can supply a num_queues value larger than the number of allocated virtqueues, causing an out-of-bounds read/write on the dev->virtqueue array. This can lead to information disclosure, memory corruption, or code execution in the vhost process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: addr may advance beyond the mmap'd region without bounds checking against mmap_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code iterates num_queues times, advancing the addr pointer by pervq_inflight_size each iteration, and writes to the memory at each offset. However, there is no validation that mmap_offset + num_queues * pervq_inflight_size <= mmap_size. A malicious guest can supply crafted values for num_queues, queue_size, mmap_size, and mmap_offset such that the pointer addr goes past the end of the mmap'd region. Subsequent writes (e.g., vq->inflight_split->desc_num = queue_size) would then write beyond the mapped area, potentially causing a crash or memory corruption. Additionally, the multiplication num_queues * pervq_inflight_size could overflow for large values.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

CPU: 1 PID: 836 Comm: handler15 Not tainted 5.18.0-rc1+ #27
...

 ? unwind_get_return_address+0x56/0xa0

 ? validate_set.constprop.0+0xc90/0xc90 [openvswitch]

 ? __mod_node_page_state+0x99/0x120
 ? __mod_lruvec_page_state+0x2e3/0x470
 ? __kasan_kmalloc_large+0x90/0xe0

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing new_acts_size due to size_t to int truncation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable new_acts_size is declared as int, but it is assigned the result of max() operating on size_t values (next_offset + req_size and ksize(*sfa) * 2). If the computed size exceeds INT_MAX, the value will be truncated when stored in the int, potentially resulting in a negative or small positive value. This corrupted size is then passed to nla_alloc_flow_actions(), which could allocate a buffer much smaller than needed. The subsequent memcpy of actions_len bytes into this undersized buffer would cause a heap buffer overflow, potentially leading to kernel memory corruption, privilege escalation, or denial of service.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in mmap_size calculation from user-controlled num_queues and queue_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables num_queues and queue_size are read directly from the message payload (user-controlled). The multiplication `num_queues * pervq_inflight_size` can overflow since num_queues is uint16_t (up to 65535) and pervq_inflight_size is uint64_t derived from queue_size. If the result wraps around, a smaller-than-expected buffer is allocated via inflight_mem_alloc, but the subsequent memset and the loop that writes into packed inflight descriptors use the original (non-overflowed or logically larger) extents, leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker controlling the vhost-user frontend can craft num_queues and queue_size values to trigger this overflow, potentially achieving code execution.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < queue_size; j++)
	inflight_packed->desc[j].next = j + 1;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when queue_size exceeds the allocated per-queue inflight descriptor array
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is no validation that queue_size (user-supplied) is within the bounds expected by the pervq_inflight_size calculation or the actual allocated region. If queue_size is very large or if the mmap_size overflowed, the loop writing to inflight_packed->desc[j] for j up to queue_size-1 can write beyond the allocated mmap region. This out-of-bounds write could corrupt adjacent memory or cause a crash, and a malicious vhost-user frontend could exploit it to achieve arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
e1000: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.
