commit_message:
Fix XkbSelectEvents() integer underflow

CVE-2020-14361 ZDI-CAN 11573

This vulnerability was discovered by:
Jan-Niklas Sohn working with Trend Micro Zero Day Initiative

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

commit_message:
LogFilePrep: add a comment to the unsafe format string.

CVE-2018-14665 also made it possible to exploit this to access
memory. With -logfile forbidden when running with elevated privileges
this is no longer an issue.

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
Reviewed-by: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
(cherry picked from commit 248d164eae27f1f310266d78e52f13f64362f81e)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in xorg-x11-server before 1.20.3. An incorrect permission check for -modulepath and -logfile options when starting Xorg. X server allows unprivileged users with the ability to log in to the system via physical console to escalate their privileges and run arbitrary code under root privileges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: User-controlled format strings passed to asprintf
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameters 'fname' and 'backup' are used directly as format strings in asprintf calls. If a caller passes attacker-controlled data for these parameters, an attacker could supply format specifiers (e.g., %s, %n, %x) to read from or write to arbitrary memory locations. Even though 'idstring' is provided as an argument, the format string itself is not validated, so mismatched or malicious specifiers could lead to crashes, information disclosure, or arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
jfs: prevent NULL deref in diFree

Add validation check for JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap to prevent a NULL deref
in diFree since diFree uses it without do any validations.
When function jfs_mount calls diMount to initialize fileset inode
allocation map, it can fail and JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap won't be
initialized. Then it calls diFreeSpecial to close fileset inode allocation
map inode and it will flow into jfs_evict_inode. Function jfs_evict_inode
just validates JFS_SBI(inode->i_sb)->ipimap, then calls diFree. diFree use
JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap directly, then it will cause a NULL deref.

Reported-by: TCS Robot <tcs_robot@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Haimin Zhang <tcs_kernel@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dave Kleikamp <dave.kleikamp@oracle.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw in diFree in fs/jfs/inode.c in Journaled File System (JFS)in the Linux kernel. This could allow a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information.

commit_message:
jfs: prevent NULL deref in diFree

Add validation check for JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap to prevent a NULL deref
in diFree since diFree uses it without do any validations.
When function jfs_mount calls diMount to initialize fileset inode
allocation map, it can fail and JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap won't be
initialized. Then it calls diFreeSpecial to close fileset inode allocation
map inode and it will flow into jfs_evict_inode. Function jfs_evict_inode
just validates JFS_SBI(inode->i_sb)->ipimap, then calls diFree. diFree use
JFS_IP(ipimap)->i_imap directly, then it will cause a NULL deref.

Reported-by: TCS Robot <tcs_robot@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Haimin Zhang <tcs_kernel@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dave Kleikamp <dave.kleikamp@oracle.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw in diFree in fs/jfs/inode.c in Journaled File System (JFS)in the Linux kernel. This could allow a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information.

commit_message:
memslot: Fix off-by-one error in group/slot boundary check

RedMemSlotInfo keeps an array of groups, and each group contains an
array of slots. Unfortunately, these checks are off by 1, they check
that the index is greater or equal to the number of elements in the
array, while these arrays are 0 based. The check should only check for
strictly greater than the number of elements.

For the group array, this is not a big issue, as these memslot groups
are created by spice-server users (eg QEMU), and the group ids used to
index that array are also generated by the spice-server user, so it
should not be possible for the guest to set them to arbitrary values.

The slot id is more problematic, as it's calculated from a QXLPHYSICAL
address, and such addresses are usually set by the guest QXL driver, so
the guest can set these to arbitrary values, including malicious values,
which are probably easy to build from the guest PCI configuration.

This patch fixes the arrays bound check, and adds a test case for this.
This fixes CVE-2019-3813.

Signed-off-by: Christophe Fergeau <cfergeau@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Frediano Ziglio <fziglio@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Spice, versions 0.5.2 through 0.14.1, are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read due to an off-by-one error in memslot_get_virt. This may lead to a denial of service, or, in the worst case, code-execution by unauthenticated attackers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one errors in group_id and slot_id boundary checks allow out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: Both boundary checks use '>' instead of '>='. If group_id equals num_memslots_groups or slot_id equals num_memslots, the checks pass but the subsequent array accesses (info->mem_slots[group_id][slot_id]) will be out of bounds. For example, if num_memslots is 8 and arrays are indexed 0-7, a slot_id of 8 passes the check but accesses beyond the allocated array. This can lead to reading/writing arbitrary memory, potentially enabling code execution or information disclosure.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when 'set' option overwrites previously percent-escaped arg1
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 'set' option handler, arg1 is unconditionally reassigned via InterpretImageProperties. However, if percent escape interpretation was already performed at the top of the function (resulting in a newly allocated string for arg1), the original allocated string is overwritten without being freed first. The cleanup code at the bottom of the function only frees the final value of arg1, leaving the first allocation leaked. Similarly, in the 'clone' option with '+clone', arg1 is overwritten with AcquireString("-1") without freeing any previously percent-escaped allocation. Repeated exploitation of this memory leak through crafted inputs could lead to resource exhaustion and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak in '+clone' when arg1 is overwritten without freeing percent-escaped version
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the '+clone' option is processed, arg1 is overwritten with AcquireString("-1"). If the percent escape interpretation at the top of the function had already allocated a new string for arg1 (because arg1n was not NULL and interpretation was enabled), this previously allocated string is leaked. The cleanup code at the end of the function will free the newly assigned string from AcquireString, but the original percent-escaped allocation is lost. This can be triggered repeatedly to exhaust memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of percent-escaped arg2 when overwritten in 'set option:' handler
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 'set' option handler for the 'option:' prefix, when processing a plus operation (IfPlusOp), arg2 is set to NULL. This overwrites any previously percent-escaped arg2 that was allocated at the top of the function. The original allocation is never freed, resulting in a memory leak. Similarly, even in the normal operation case, the re-interpretation of arg2 via InterpretImageProperties overwrites the initial percent-escaped allocation. An attacker could trigger this repeatedly to cause memory exhaustion and denial of service.

commit_message:
Bug 701815: avoid uninitialised bytes being >7, which broke indexing.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=jetp3852 ../bug-701815.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap/stack buffer overflow when padding end_data with 7 zero bytes past the valid data region
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `data` buffer is declared as `byte data[DATA_SIZE]`. The variable `end_data` is set by scanning backwards from `data + line_size` (where `line_size <= DATA_SIZE`). After finding the last non-zero byte, `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` writes 7 bytes of padding. However, `end_data` can point up to `data + DATA_SIZE` (if no trailing zeros are found and `line_size == DATA_SIZE`). In that case, `memset` writes 7 bytes beyond the end of the `data` array, corrupting adjacent stack memory. This can lead to stack corruption, control-flow hijacking, or denial of service. Even when `line_size < DATA_SIZE`, if `end_data` is within 7 bytes of `data + DATA_SIZE`, the write still overflows.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read of uninitialized data beyond line_size in the transpose loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: The transpose loop iterates over the full DATA_SIZE (LINE_SIZE * 8) bytes of the `data` buffer, reading 8 bytes at a time via `dp[0]` through `dp[7]`. However, only `line_size` bytes were copied into `data` via `gdev_prn_copy_scan_lines`. When `line_size < DATA_SIZE`, the loop reads uninitialized stack memory beyond the valid scan line data. These uninitialized values are used as array indices into the `spr40`, `spr8`, and `spr2` lookup tables (each of size 8), and since byte values can be 0-255, this results in massive out-of-bounds reads on those arrays, potentially leaking sensitive stack/memory contents into the output stream.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No bounds checking on destination buffer allows heap/stack buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function writes left_width + (num_tiles * src_width) + right_width bytes into dest_strip without any validation that the destination buffer is large enough. None of the parameters (left_width, num_tiles, src_width, right_width) are validated for being non-negative or for their combined size fitting within the destination. A caller supplying incorrect or malicious values (e.g., a very large num_tiles or negative left_offset) can cause writes far beyond the allocated dest_strip buffer, leading to arbitrary memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from src_strip when left_offset + left_width exceeds source buffer size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The first memcpy reads left_width bytes starting at src_strip + left_offset. There is no validation that left_offset is within bounds of the source buffer, or that left_offset + left_width <= src_width. If left_offset is negative or left_offset + left_width exceeds the source allocation, this results in an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow due to no validation of sizes against destination buffer capacity
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function performs multiple memcpy operations into dest_strip without any validation that the total bytes written (left_width + num_tiles * src_width + right_width) fit within the destination buffer. If a caller provides inconsistent or attacker-influenced parameters (e.g., large num_tiles, negative values reinterpreted as large unsigned sizes for memcpy's size_t parameter, or a large right_width), the writes will overflow the dest_strip buffer. This can lead to arbitrary memory corruption, code execution, or denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from src_strip when left_offset + left_width exceeds source buffer size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function reads from src_strip + left_offset for left_width bytes without validating that left_offset + left_width does not exceed the bounds of src_strip (whose known dimension is src_width). If left_offset + left_width > src_width, this results in an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. Similarly, if right_width > src_width, the final memcpy reads beyond the source buffer.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0360: crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored

Problem:    Crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored.
Solution:   Do not check breakpoint for non-existing line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0360.

commit_message:
mtab: handle ENOSPC/EFBIG condition properly when altering mtab

It's possible that when mount.cifs goes to append the mtab that there
won't be enough space to do so, and the mntent won't be appended to the
file in its entirety.

Add a my_endmntent routine that will fflush and then fsync the FILE if
that succeeds. If either fails then it will truncate the file back to
its provided size. It will then call endmntent unconditionally.

Have add_mtab call fstat on the opened mtab file in order to get the
size of the file before it has been appended. Assuming that that
succeeds, use my_endmntent to ensure that the file is not corrupted
before closing it. It's possible that we'll have a small race window
where the mtab is incorrect, but it should be quickly corrected.

This was reported some time ago as CVE-2011-1678:

    http://openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2011/03/04/9

...and it seems to fix the reproducer that I was able to come up with.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Suresh Jayaraman <sjayaraman@suse.de>
cve_desc: smbfs in Samba 3.5.8 and earlier attempts to use (1) mount.cifs to append to the /etc/mtab file and (2) umount.cifs to append to the /etc/mtab.tmp file without first checking whether resource limits would interfere, which allows local users to trigger corruption of the /etc/mtab file via a process with a small RLIMIT_FSIZE value, a related issue to CVE-2011-1089.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3884: crash when clearing the argument list while using it

Problem:    Crash when clearing the argument list while using it.
Solution:   Lock the argument list for ":all".
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Read

code_snippets:
(void)do_ecmd(0, alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i]), NULL, NULL,
		      ECMD_ONE,
		      ((buf_hide(curwin->w_buffer)
			   || bufIsChanged(curwin->w_buffer)) ? ECMD_HIDE : 0)
						       + ECMD_OLDBUF, curwin);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access on argument list due to autocommands changing its size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `opened` array and `count` are sized based on ARGCOUNT at the start of the function. However, autocommands triggered by `do_ecmd()`, `win_close()`, `autowrite()`, and other calls can modify the argument list, shrinking `alist->al_ga.ga_len` to be smaller than the original `opened_len`/`count`. In the second main loop, `AARGLIST(alist)[i]` is accessed without rechecking whether `i` is still within `alist->al_ga.ga_len`, leading to an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, if autocommands grow the argument list, the `opened[i]` accesses in the first loop (where `i` iterates up to `opened_len`) are safe, but the weight-based writes `opened[i] = (char_u)weight` rely on `i < opened_len` which is checked indirectly. An attacker who can trigger specific autocommands (e.g., via modelines or crafted autocmd configurations) could cause heap out-of-bounds reads, potentially leading to information disclosure or code execution.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220311080208.45047-1-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marc Kleine-Budde <mkl@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: mcba_usb_start_xmit in drivers/net/can/usb/mcba_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.17.1 has a double free.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer overflow via unsanitized CAN frame DLC/len used as memcpy size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The cf->len field is copied directly into usb_msg.dlc and then used as the size argument to memcpy into usb_msg.data, which is a fixed-size buffer (typically 8 bytes for CAN). While can_dropped_invalid_skb validates the skb size matches a can_frame struct, it does not necessarily guarantee that cf->len is bounded to CAN_MAX_DLEN (8). A crafted CAN frame with cf->len > 8 would cause memcpy to write beyond the bounds of usb_msg.data, corrupting adjacent fields in the stack-allocated usb_msg structure and potentially other stack data. This could be exploited by a local user with access to the CAN socket to achieve stack corruption, denial of service, or potentially code execution in kernel context.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3884: crash when clearing the argument list while using it

Problem:    Crash when clearing the argument list while using it.
Solution:   Lock the argument list for ":all".
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Read

code_snippets:
(void)do_ecmd(0, alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i]), NULL, NULL,
		      ECMD_ONE,
		      ((buf_hide(curwin->w_buffer)
			   || bufIsChanged(curwin->w_buffer)) ? ECMD_HIDE : 0)
						       + ECMD_OLDBUF, curwin);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on argument list when autocommands shrink it during iteration
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `opened` array and `count`/`opened_len` are sized based on the initial `ARGCOUNT`. The code acknowledges that autocommands can modify the argument list ('We still have to watch out for its size to be changed'). However, in the second main loop (`for (i = 0; i < count ...)`), when the code accesses `AARGLIST(alist)[i]` via `alist_name(&AARGLIST(alist)[i])` inside the `do_ecmd` call, there is no check that `i` is still within the current bounds of `alist->al_ga.ga_len`. Autocommands triggered by `win_split()`, `do_ecmd()`, or other operations earlier in the loop iteration could remove entries from the argument list, shrinking `al_ga.ga_len` below `count`. This leads to an out-of-bounds heap read on the argument list array, which could cause a crash or potentially leak sensitive heap memory. An attacker who can influence autocommands (e.g., via a crafted modeline or vimrc) could trigger this condition.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0360: crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored

Problem:    Crash when invalid line number on :for is ignored.
Solution:   Do not check breakpoint for non-existing line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0360.

commit_message: don't expand string case folds to alternatives if code length == 1 and byte length is same
cve_desc: Oniguruma through 6.9.3, as used in PHP 7.3.x and other products, has a heap-based buffer over-read in str_lower_case_match in regexec.c.

code_snippets: r = expand_case_fold_string_alt(n, items, p, len, end, reg, &prev_node);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to unchecked character length from enclen()
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls `enclen(reg->enc, p)` to determine the byte length of the character at pointer `p`, but does not verify that `p + len <= end` before using `len` to read from the buffer (e.g., in `onig_node_str_cat(snode, p, p + len)` and `expand_case_fold_string_alt`). If the input buffer is truncated or contains a malformed multi-byte character near the end of the string, `enclen` may return a length that extends past the `end` boundary. This leads to an out-of-bounds heap read, which can cause information disclosure or a crash. This pattern has been the root cause of several Oniguruma CVEs (e.g., CVE-2019-19246).

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

mtab: handle ENOSPC/EFBIG condition properly when altering mtab

It's possible that when mount.cifs goes to append the mtab that there

that succeeds. If either fails then it will truncate the file back to
its provided size. It will then call endmntent unconditionally.

Have add_mtab call fstat on the opened mtab file in order to get the
size of the file before it has been appended. Assuming that that

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error in null terminator placement leaves a byte uninitialized and can write out of bounds
vulnerabilities.long_description: The null terminator is written at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. Since `llen` represents the number of bytes copied into `line`, the correct index for the null terminator is `llen`, not `llen + 1`. This off-by-one means: (1) one byte of `line` is left uninitialized between the copied data and the null terminator, leading to processing of stale data, and (2) when the overflow check triggers and sets `llen = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1`, after `llen += gopherState->len` the value becomes `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, and writing at `line[llen + 1]` = `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]` is a one-byte out-of-bounds write on the stack-allocated buffer of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the HTML_CSO_RESULT handler, `strtok` is called three times. Only `result` (the third call) is checked for NULL before use. If the input line doesn't contain enough ':' or '\n' delimiters, `s_code` or `s_recno` can be NULL, and the subsequent `atoi(s_code)` or `atoi(s_recno)` calls will dereference a null pointer, causing a crash. A malicious gopher server can send crafted CSO response lines to trigger this.

code_snippets: snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector + 5), html_quote(name));
snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, " %s\n", icon_url, host, gtype, escaped_selector, html_quote(name));
vulnerabilities.short_description: Cross-site scripting via unescaped host and port values in generated HTML
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `host` and `port` values are extracted directly from the gopher server's response and inserted into HTML output without HTML entity escaping. While `name` is passed through `html_quote()`, `host` is inserted directly into href attributes and HTML content. A malicious gopher server could include specially crafted host values containing characters like `"` to break out of the attribute and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript, leading to cross-site scripting when the generated page is rendered in a user's browser.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error in null terminator placement leaves uninitialized byte and potential buffer overwrite
vulnerabilities.long_description: The null terminator is placed at `llen + 1` instead of `llen`. Since `llen` bytes of data are copied into `line` (starting at index 0), the valid data ends at index `llen - 1`, and the null terminator should be at index `llen`. Placing it at `llen + 1` leaves one uninitialized byte in the string and, when `llen` equals `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` (after truncation), the write at `llen + 1` goes one byte past the buffer boundary, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Format string argument count mismatch causes reading of uninitialized stack memory
vulnerabilities.long_description: The format string contains five format specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only three arguments are passed (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). The `%c` and the second `%s` will read whatever values happen to be on the stack, leading to information disclosure or a crash. An attacker controlling a malicious gopher server could craft a response with gtype == GOPHER_WWW to trigger this code path.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when strtok returns NULL for s_code or s_recno
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code only checks whether `result` (the third strtok call) is NULL before using all three values. If `s_code` or `s_recno` are NULL (because the input line doesn't contain enough ':' delimiters), they are passed to `atoi()` without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash. A malicious gopher server can send a CSO result line starting with '-' but missing the expected ':' delimiters to trigger this.

commit_message: Fix oobread in symbols header parsing ##bin
cve_desc: Heap Buffer Overflow in parseDragons in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of allocated buffer `b` on early return paths after failed r_buf_read_at calls
vulnerabilities.long_description: The buffer `b` is allocated via malloc() but is not freed before returning NULL when r_buf_read_at fails. This occurs in two places: the first r_buf_read_at check and the second r_buf_read_at check inside the magicCombo retry block. Repeated calls to this function with crafted input could cause unbounded memory consumption leading to denial of service.

code_snippets: static RCoreSymCacheElement *parseDragons(RBinFile *bf, RBuffer *buf, int off, int bits, R_OWN char *file_name) {
vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of R_OWN `file_name` on all early return NULL paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameter `file_name` is marked R_OWN, meaning this function takes ownership and is responsible for freeing it. However, all early `return NULL` paths (size checks, malloc failure, and the r_buf_read_at failures) return without freeing `file_name`, causing a memory leak. An attacker providing a malformed binary could trigger these early returns repeatedly, leaking memory each time.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap/buffer overflow when rewriting visual range with plus command
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `has_visual_range` and `use_plus_cmd` are both true, the code rewrites the command buffer in-place. The original buffer holds "'<,'>" (5 bytes) + the rest of the command (len bytes) + NUL (1 byte), totaling len + 6 bytes. After the transformation, `mch_memmove` copies `len` bytes to `orig_cmd`, then `STRCPY` appends "'<,'>+" (6 characters + NUL = 7 bytes) at `orig_cmd + len`, writing a total of `len + 7` bytes. This exceeds the original buffer size by 1 byte, causing a classic off-by-one write overflow. An attacker who can control command input in ex mode with a visual range selection could trigger this overflow to corrupt adjacent heap metadata or data, potentially leading to code execution.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of `buf` on multiple early return paths after allocation.
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated via `malloc`, there are several early `return` statements (when ESIL is NULL, when `spname` is NULL, when `pcname` is NULL/empty) that do not call `free(buf)` before returning. This causes a memory leak each time these error paths are taken. In a long-running application or when this function is called repeatedly, this can lead to significant memory consumption and potential denial of service.

code_snippets: if (!r_anal_op (core->anal, &op, cur, buf + i, iend - i, R_ANAL_OP_MASK_ESIL | R_ANAL_OP_MASK_VAL | R_ANAL_OP_MASK_HINT))
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when assigning ut64 subtraction result to `int iend`.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `iend` is declared as `int`, but it is assigned the result of `end - start`, where both are `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). If the difference exceeds INT_MAX but wraps to a small positive int value (e.g., when the upper bits cause truncation), the `iend < 0` check and `iend > MAX_SCAN_SIZE` check could both pass, resulting in a too-small buffer allocation relative to the actual range. This leads to a heap buffer overflow when `r_io_read_at` writes `iend + 1` bytes or when `r_anal_op` reads from `buf + i` with an incorrect remaining length.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on `buf` when `i` approaches or exceeds `iend`.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `i` is incremented by `minopsize - 1` or `op.size - 1` before the bounds check at the `repeat` label (`if (i >= iend) break`). Meanwhile, the `r_anal_op` call uses `buf + i` with length `iend - i`. Since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned) and `iend` is `int`, if `i` exceeds `iend`, the expression `iend - i` wraps to a very large value due to implicit unsigned conversion, allowing `r_anal_op` to read well beyond the allocated buffer. The check `if (i > iend) goto repeat` is placed before `r_anal_op`, but after the `i += minopsize - 1` increment from a previous iteration, `i` could still be equal to `iend`, resulting in a zero-length buffer passed to the analysis function, or `i` could bypass the check via the `repeat` path logic.

commit_message:
ieee802154: atusb: fix use-after-free at disconnect

The disconnect callback was accessing the hardware-descriptor private
data after having having freed it.

Fixes: 7490b008d123 ("ieee802154: add support for atusb transceiver")
Cc: stable <stable@vger.kernel.org>     # 4.2
Cc: Alexander Aring <alex.aring@gmail.com>
Reported-by: syzbot+f4509a9138a1472e7e80@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Johan Hovold <johan@kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Schmidt <stefan@datenfreihafen.org>
cve_desc: In the Linux kernel before 5.3.6, there is a use-after-free bug that can be caused by a malicious USB device in the drivers/net/ieee802154/atusb.c driver, aka CID-7fd25e6fc035.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when formatted string exceeds buffer size
vulnerabilities.long_description: vsnprintf returns the number of characters that *would* have been written if the buffer were large enough. If the formatted output exceeds 256 bytes, `len` will be larger than the actual data in `print_buffer`. The subsequent loop uses `len` to control iteration and accesses `ptr` (which points into the 256-byte stack buffer) beyond its bounds, causing an out-of-bounds read. This could leak sensitive stack data to the error output or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double va_start invocation without intervening va_end causes undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: va_start is called twice consecutively on the same va_list `ap` without a va_end in between. Per the C standard, calling va_start again without first calling va_end results in undefined behavior. This could corrupt the stack or produce incorrect formatting results depending on the platform and compiler.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

commit_message:
Fix oob write in dyldcache

When the individual n_slide_infos were too high, the sum would overflow
and too few entries would be allocated.
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from dyld cache files. A user opening a malicious dyld cache file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 556ca2f9eef01ec0f4a76d1fbacfcf3a87a44810 contains a patch.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Double va_start invocation without intervening va_end causes undefined behavior
vulnerabilities.long_description: va_start is called twice on the same va_list object without an intervening va_end. Per the C standard, calling va_start a second time without first calling va_end results in undefined behavior. Depending on the platform and compiler, this could corrupt the stack, produce garbage output, or cause a crash. While the practical impact varies, it is a real bug that can lead to unpredictable behavior.

code_snippets:
ptr += cut+1; /* skip the space too */
        len -= cut;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one in length tracking can cause out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: When vsnprintf returns a value >= sizeof(print_buffer) (i.e., the output was truncated), 'len' is set to the number of characters that *would* have been written, which is larger than what is actually in print_buffer. The loop then iterates using this inflated length, causing reads past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer via ptr, fwrite, and fputs. This is an out-of-bounds read that could leak stack data or crash the process. Additionally, the line 'len -= cut' (instead of 'len -= cut+1') means the pointer advances by cut+1 but len only decreases by cut, creating a mismatch that can also lead to over-reading the buffer.

commit_message:
LogFilePrep: add a comment to the unsafe format string.

CVE-2018-14665 also made it possible to exploit this to access
memory. With -logfile forbidden when running with elevated privileges
this is no longer an issue.

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
Reviewed-by: Adam Jackson <ajax@redhat.com>
(cherry picked from commit 248d164eae27f1f310266d78e52f13f64362f81e)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in xorg-x11-server before 1.20.3. An incorrect permission check for -modulepath and -logfile options when starting Xorg. X server allows unprivileged users with the ability to log in to the system via physical console to escalate their privileges and run arbitrary code under root privileges.

vulnerabilities.short_description: User-controlled format string passed to asprintf
vulnerabilities.long_description: The parameters 'fname' and 'backup' are used directly as format strings in asprintf calls. Although the comment acknowledges this is user-controlled and claims the code should never run with elevated privileges, if that assumption is violated (or if an attacker can influence these parameters), they could supply format specifiers like %n or %s to read/write arbitrary memory, leading to information disclosure or code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'suffix' if second asprintf fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the first asprintf (for 'suffix') succeeds but the second asprintf (for 'oldLog') fails, 'suffix' is never freed before FatalError is called. Depending on whether FatalError terminates the process or not, this constitutes a memory leak. If FatalError does not terminate, subsequent code could also dereference an uninitialized 'oldLog' pointer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: TOCTOU race condition between stat and rename
vulnerabilities.long_description: There is a time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race between the stat() call that checks if logFileName is a regular file and the subsequent rename() call. An attacker could replace logFileName with a symlink between these two operations, potentially causing the rename to operate on a different file than intended, leading to arbitrary file manipulation.

commit_message:
memslot: Fix off-by-one error in group/slot boundary check

RedMemSlotInfo keeps an array of groups, and each group contains an
array of slots. Unfortunately, these checks are off by 1, they check
that the index is greater or equal to the number of elements in the
array, while these arrays are 0 based. The check should only check for
strictly greater than the number of elements.

For the group array, this is not a big issue, as these memslot groups
are created by spice-server users (eg QEMU), and the group ids used to
index that array are also generated by the spice-server user, so it
should not be possible for the guest to set them to arbitrary values.

The slot id is more problematic, as it's calculated from a QXLPHYSICAL
address, and such addresses are usually set by the guest QXL driver, so
the guest can set these to arbitrary values, including malicious values,
which are probably easy to build from the guest PCI configuration.

This patch fixes the arrays bound check, and adds a test case for this.
This fixes CVE-2019-3813.

Signed-off-by: Christophe Fergeau <cfergeau@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Frediano Ziglio <fziglio@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Spice, versions 0.5.2 through 0.14.1, are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read due to an off-by-one error in memslot_get_virt. This may lead to a denial of service, or, in the worst case, code-execution by unauthenticated attackers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when adding address_delta to the virtual address
vulnerabilities.long_description: The addition of slot->address_delta to h_virt is performed without overflow checking. If address_delta is crafted (e.g., a large or negative value), the resulting h_virt could wrap around, potentially pointing to an unintended memory location. The subsequent memslot_validate_virt check may pass if the wrapped value falls within the valid slot range, allowing an attacker to read or write arbitrary memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow when h_virt + add_size is computed during validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The memslot_validate_virt function presumably checks that the range [h_virt, h_virt + add_size) is within the slot bounds. Since h_virt is an unsigned long and add_size is a uint32_t, if their sum overflows, the bounds check could be bypassed, allowing access outside the memory slot. A guest supplying a large add_size or a virtual address near the top of the address space could exploit this to achieve out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message: don't expand string case folds to alternatives if code length == 1 and byte length is same
cve_desc: Oniguruma through 6.9.3, as used in PHP 7.3.x and other products, has a heap-based buffer over-read in str_lower_case_match in regexec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in alt_num multiplication bypasses expansion threshold check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `alt_num` is declared as `int` and is multiplied by `(n + 1)` on each iteration where case-fold alternatives are expanded. If a crafted regex pattern causes multiple iterations through this branch with sufficiently large `n` values, `alt_num` can overflow. A signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C, but practically the value could wrap to a small positive number or a negative number, either of which would fail the `> THRESHOLD_CASE_FOLD_ALT_FOR_EXPANSION` check. This bypasses the intended limit on the number of alternatives generated, potentially leading to exponential node expansion, excessive memory consumption, and denial of service (ReDoS-style resource exhaustion).

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3610: crash when ModeChanged triggered too early

Problem:    Crash when ModeChanged triggered too early.
Solution:   Trigger ModeChanged after setting VIsual.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table due to missing bounds checks on 'bytes' index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable 'bytes' is used as an offset into 'directory_table' for multiple memcpy calls, but it is never validated against the actual allocated size of directory_table. The value of 'size' is derived from inode data ((*i)->data + bytes) which can be attacker-controlled in a crafted filesystem image. A malicious squashfs image could set data values that cause 'bytes' to exceed the directory_table buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads. This could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when computing 'size' from attacker-controlled inode data
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'size' variable is computed as the sum of (*i)->data (from the filesystem inode) and 'bytes'. Both are int types. If (*i)->data is a large value from a crafted filesystem, this addition can overflow, resulting in a small or negative 'size' value. This could cause the while(bytes < size) loop to either skip entirely or iterate with incorrect bounds, leading to unexpected behavior or bypassing the intended loop termination.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential buffer overflow when copying directory entry name into fixed-size dir_ent.name field
vulnerabilities.long_description: The directory entry name (up to SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN bytes) is copied via strcpy into dir->dirs[dir->dir_count].name, which is likely a fixed-size buffer in the dir_ent structure. While dire->size is checked to be less than SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN, there is no check that it fits within the destination buffer's actual size. If the name field in struct dir_ent is smaller than SQUASHFS_NAME_LEN+2 bytes, a crafted filesystem with long filenames could overflow this buffer, potentially enabling code execution.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Multiple early returns leak allocated memory (buf, spname, pcname).
vulnerabilities.long_description: After `buf` is allocated, several early return paths exist that do not free it. Specifically: (1) If ESIL initialization fails after `buf` is allocated, the function returns without freeing `buf`. (2) If `kspname` is empty, the function returns without freeing `buf`. (3) After `spname` is allocated via `strdup`, if `kpcname` is NULL/empty, the function returns without freeing `buf` or `spname`. These memory leaks can be triggered repeatedly by an attacker controlling the analysis state, potentially leading to resource exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when assigning ut64 subtraction result to int variable `iend`.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `iend` is declared as `int`, but it is assigned the result of `end - start` where both are `ut64` (unsigned 64-bit). If `end - start` produces a value larger than INT_MAX but that happens to truncate to a positive int (e.g., a value like 0x100000000 + small_positive truncates to small_positive on 32-bit int), the `iend < 0` safety check is bypassed, and `iend` holds a much smaller value than the actual range. This leads to an undersized `buf` allocation, and the subsequent `r_io_read_at` reading `iend + 1` bytes would be correct for the truncated size but the loop iterates based on addresses derived from `end`, potentially reading past the buffer boundary. Conversely, if the truncation results in a value that passes the MAX_SCAN_SIZE check but is inconsistent with the actual range, buffer over-reads in `buf + i` within the analysis loop become possible.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on `buf` when `i` approaches or exceeds `iend`.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check `if (i > iend)` uses strict greater-than, so when `i == iend`, the code proceeds to call `r_anal_op` with `buf + i` and length `iend - i = 0`. If `r_anal_op` doesn't handle a zero-length buffer safely, it could read past the allocated buffer. Additionally, after `r_anal_op` fails, `i += minopsize - 1` (typically 3) is added, and then in the `repeat` block, `i` is incremented again by the loop. This can cause `i` to skip past the `i > iend` check on the next iteration if timing is unfavorable, leading to `buf + i` accessing beyond the allocated `iend + 2` bytes.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

CPU: 1 PID: 836 Comm: handler15 Not tainted 5.18.0-rc1+ #27
...

 ? validate_set.constprop.0+0xc90/0xc90 [openvswitch]

 ? unwind_next_frame+0x991/0x1e40
 ? __mod_node_page_state+0x99/0x120
 ? __mod_lruvec_page_state+0x2e3/0x470
 ? __kasan_kmalloc_large+0x90/0xe0
 ovs_nla_copy_actions+0x1b4/0x2c0 [openvswitch]
 ovs_flow_cmd_new+0x3cd/0xb10 [openvswitch]
 ...

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when assigning size_t result to int new_acts_size can bypass MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE check and cause undersized allocation.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `new_acts_size` is declared as `int`, but it is assigned the result of `max(next_offset + req_size, ksize(*sfa) * 2)`, which are `size_t` expressions. If the computed size exceeds INT_MAX, the value is truncated when stored in the `int`, potentially resulting in a small or negative value. A negative `new_acts_size` would fail the `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` comparison (signed vs unsigned comparison promotes to unsigned, but if treated as signed, a negative value is less than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE), potentially bypassing the size limit check. This could lead to `nla_alloc_flow_actions` allocating a buffer that is too small, and the subsequent `memcpy` of `(*sfa)->actions_len` bytes would overflow the heap buffer, leading to heap corruption, denial of service, or potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned underflow in size comparison if next_offset exceeds ksize(*sfa) causes the check to be trivially satisfied, skipping reallocation.
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `ksize(*sfa) - next_offset` involves subtracting an `int` from a `size_t`. If `next_offset` (the current used size) somehow exceeds `ksize(*sfa)` (the actual allocated size), the subtraction wraps around to a very large unsigned value due to unsigned arithmetic. This would make the condition `req_size <= (very_large_value)` true, causing execution to jump to `out` without reallocating. The function would then write the nlattr at an offset beyond the actual allocated buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Bug 701815: avoid uninitialised bytes being >7, which broke indexing.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=jetp3852 ../bug-701815.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when scan line bytes exceed valid lookup table index range (0-7).
vulnerabilities.long_description: The lookup tables spr40, spr8, and spr2 each contain only 8 elements (indices 0-7), but they are indexed by raw byte values from the scan line data (dp[0] through dp[7]), which can range from 0 to 255. While the memset at the start addresses uninitialized memory, the actual pixel data copied by gdev_prn_copy_scan_lines is not validated to be in the range 0-7. If any byte in the scan data is >= 8, the code reads past the end of the static arrays, resulting in an out-of-bounds read. This can leak stack or static memory contents, potentially cause a crash, or produce unpredictable output. A maliciously crafted input document that causes pixel values outside the expected 3-bit range could trigger this vulnerability.

commit_message:
ieee802154: atusb: fix use-after-free at disconnect

The disconnect callback was accessing the hardware-descriptor private
data after having having freed it.

Fixes: 7490b008d123 ("ieee802154: add support for atusb transceiver")
Cc: stable <stable@vger.kernel.org>     # 4.2
Cc: Alexander Aring <alex.aring@gmail.com>
Reported-by: syzbot+f4509a9138a1472e7e80@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Johan Hovold <johan@kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Schmidt <stefan@datenfreihafen.org>
cve_desc: In the Linux kernel before 5.3.6, there is a use-after-free bug that can be caused by a malicious USB device in the drivers/net/ieee802154/atusb.c driver, aka CID-7fd25e6fc035.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: atusb structure accessed after ieee802154_free_hw frees it
vulnerabilities.long_description: ieee802154_free_hw(atusb->hw) frees the hw object along with its private data (atusb). After this call, atusb is a dangling pointer. The subsequent accesses to atusb->usb_dev in usb_put_dev() constitute a use-after-free. The usb_set_intfdata call also accesses freed memory indirectly through the ordering. To fix this, usb_put_dev and usb_set_intfdata should be called before ieee802154_free_hw, or the usb_dev pointer should be saved to a local variable before freeing.
