Reviewed-by: Alistair Francis <alistair.francis@wdc.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message: annotate: don't allow everyone to write shared server entries
cve_desc: Cyrus IMAP before 3.2.7, and 3.3.x and 3.4.x before 3.4.1, allows remote authenticated users to bypass intended access restrictions on server annotations and consequently cause replication to stall.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal can lead to NULL pointer dereference
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the capath referral loop (the `else if (ret)` branch handling `req_rlm != NULL`), the return value of `krb5_make_principal` is not checked. If this call fails, `sp` (which was previously freed and set to NULL) will remain NULL, and the subsequent `krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...)` call and `goto server_lookup` will dereference a NULL pointer. This could cause a denial of service crash of the KDC. An attacker could trigger this by sending a TGS-REQ for a cross-realm krbtgt principal that causes the capath lookup to enter this code path under memory pressure or other failure conditions.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of PA_S4U2Self structure on error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `_krb5_s4u2self_to_checksumdata` fails, the code jumps to `goto out` without first calling `free_PA_S4U2Self(&self)`. The `self` structure was decoded from attacker-controlled padata and may contain allocated memory. Repeated exploitation of this error path could lead to memory exhaustion and denial of service against the KDC. The same issue exists if `krb5_crypto_init` fails, though that path does call `free_PA_S4U2Self`.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of tp and tpn when both S4U2Self and constrained delegation paths execute
vulnerabilities.long_description: The S4U2Self code path allocates new `tp` and `tpn` values. If the constrained delegation code path also executes (the two conditions are not mutually exclusive), it overwrites `tp` and `tpn` with new allocations without freeing the previous values from the S4U2Self path. The cleanup code only frees the final `tp`/`tpn`. An attacker who can trigger both code paths repeatedly could cause memory exhaustion of the KDC process.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when accessing dec->cdef without a NULL check in the CMAP processing branch
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP box is present, the code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef (via `cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef`) without first checking whether dec->cdef is non-NULL. If the input file contains a CMAP box but no CDEF box, dec->cdef will be NULL, leading to a null pointer dereference. An attacker can craft a JP2 file with a CMAP and PCLR box but no CDEF box to trigger a crash, potentially causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when jas_alloc2 for lutents fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of jas_alloc2() when allocating lutents is not checked for NULL. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to a large numlutents value in a crafted file), the subsequent loop will dereference a NULL pointer, leading to a crash. An attacker could craft a JP2 file that triggers this allocation failure to cause a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds component reference for direct-mapped CMAP channels
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP entry has map type JP2_CMAP_DIRECT, the code sets `dec->chantocmptlut[channo] = channo`. However, channo could be greater than or equal to the number of image components, causing later use of this LUT entry to access an out-of-bounds component index. The validation loop only checks cmptno for validity but does not validate that direct-mapped channel numbers are valid component indices. This could lead to out-of-bounds reads or writes when the LUT is later used to set component types or access component data.

commit_message:
Fix XkbSelectEvents() integer underflow

CVE-2020-14361 ZDI-CAN 11573

This vulnerability was discovered by:
Jan-Niklas Sohn working with Trend Micro Zero Day Initiative

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read due to mismatch between bounds check size and actual consumed size for 1-byte event types
vulnerabilities.long_description: For event types XkbBellNotify, XkbActionMessage, and XkbCompatMapNotify, size is set to 1. The bounds check verifies dataLeft >= (1*2) = 2 bytes are available. However, in the else branch (since size is neither 2 nor 4), size is then reassigned to 2. The subsequent pointer advance and dataLeft subtraction use the new size value, consuming (2*2) = 4 bytes instead of the 2 bytes that were validated. This means the code can read up to 2 bytes beyond what was checked, causing an out-of-bounds heap read. Additionally, if dataLeft was 2 or 3, subtracting 4 from the unsigned dataLeft causes an integer underflow, wrapping to a very large value, which allows the loop to continue processing memory far beyond the legitimate request buffer. A malicious client can craft a request to trigger this, potentially leaking sensitive heap data or causing a crash.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Logic error using && instead of || allows processing of too-short skb buffers
vulnerabilities.long_description: The guard condition uses logical AND (&&) instead of logical OR (||). This means the check only rejects the packet when BOTH conditions are true (length too short AND wrong tag). If the length is too short but the first byte happens to match NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, execution continues, leading to out-of-bounds reads on skb->data in all subsequent accesses (e.g., skb->data[1], skb->data[2], skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 2], etc.). Additionally, if skb->len < 2, the expression `skb->len - 2` passed to devm_kzalloc underflows (unsigned), potentially causing a massive allocation or subsequent heap overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on skb->data due to missing bounds check against skb->len
vulnerabilities.long_description: After validating that transaction->aid_len fits within sizeof(transaction->aid), the code accesses skb->data at offsets transaction->aid_len + 2 and transaction->aid_len + 3 without verifying that these offsets are within skb->len. A crafted packet with a valid-looking aid_len but a short skb buffer will cause out-of-bounds heap reads, potentially leaking kernel memory or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak on error paths after devm_kzalloc allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the function returns -EINVAL or -EPROTO after the devm_kzalloc allocation, the allocated transaction memory is not explicitly freed. While devm_kzalloc ties the allocation to the device lifetime, the transaction object will persist until the device is removed, effectively leaking memory. An attacker sending repeated malformed packets can exhaust kernel memory.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Failure to drop privileges is not treated as fatal, allowing continued execution with elevated privileges
vulnerabilities.long_description: When setuid() or setgid() fails, the function logs an error but continues execution (except in the narrow case where EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is defined and errno is EAGAIN). The cached values current_user.euid and current_user.egid are unconditionally updated to the unprivileged values regardless of whether the system calls succeeded, meaning subsequent privilege checks using these cached values will incorrectly believe privileges were dropped. This allows the process to continue running with elevated privileges while believing it is unprivileged.

vulnerabilities.short_description: setuid() is called before setgid(), potentially preventing gid change after uid privileges are dropped
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls setuid() before setgid(). Once the process has permanently dropped its effective UID to an unprivileged user via setuid(), it may no longer have the privileges necessary to call setgid() to change its group ID. The correct order is to call setgid() first (while still privileged), then setuid(). This misordering can result in the process retaining its original (potentially privileged) group ID while only the user ID is dropped.

commit_message: annotate: don't allow everyone to write shared server entries
cve_desc: Cyrus IMAP before 3.2.7, and 3.3.x and 3.4.x before 3.4.1, allows remote authenticated users to bypass intended access restrictions on server annotations and consequently cause replication to stall.

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on control_bytes pointer with no bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `control_bytes` pointer is set to a position within the buffer, then accessed via `control_bytes[0]` and incremented with `control_bytes++` inside the tag-parsing loop without any validation that it remains within the bounds of `buf->data`. If `tagx->tags_count` is large or multiple `control_byte == 1` entries exist, `control_bytes` can be advanced past the end of the buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds read. An attacker crafting a malicious MOBI file could trigger information disclosure or a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in multiplication of attacker-controlled tag value counts
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `ptagx[i].value_count * ptagx[i].tag_value_count` multiplies two uint32_t values derived from parsed file data. If both values are large, the multiplication can overflow, wrapping around to a small or zero value. This could cause the loop to iterate an incorrect number of times. Additionally, when the result is assigned to `size_t count`, on a 32-bit platform this remains overflowed, potentially leading to fewer reads than expected and leaving uninitialized tag data, or on other paths causing excessive iterations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer maxlen not restored on multiple error return paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function saves `buf->maxlen` and overwrites it with a restricted value. However, several error return paths (e.g., MOBI_MALLOC_FAILED returns, MOBI_DATA_CORRUPT after label_length check) do not restore `buf->maxlen` to `buf_maxlen` before returning. This leaves the buffer in an inconsistent state with a truncated maxlen, which could cause subsequent buffer operations to behave incorrectly — potentially leading to out-of-bounds access or denial of service in callers that continue using the buffer.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak on allocation failure: previously allocated tagvalues and label not freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `malloc` fails for `tags[i].tagvalues`, the function frees `ptagx` but does not free previously allocated `tags[j].tagvalues` (for j < i), the `tags` array itself, or `indx->entries[entry_number].label`. Similarly, other error paths after allocating the label do not free it. In a memory-constrained environment, repeated triggering of these paths (e.g., via crafted files) can cause memory exhaustion and denial of service.

commit_message:
LZWDecodeCompat(): fix potential index-out-of-bounds write. Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2780 / CVE-2018-8905

The fix consists in using the similar code LZWDecode() to validate we
don't write outside of the output buffer.
cve_desc: In LibTIFF 4.0.9, a heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the function LZWDecodeCompat in tif_lzw.c via a crafted TIFF file, as demonstrated by tiff2ps.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds access due to missing bounds check on LZW code value before table lookup
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the main decoding loop, after reading a code that is not CODE_EOI or CODE_CLEAR, the code computes `codep = sp->dec_codetab + code` without validating that `code` is within the bounds of the `dec_codetab` array (size CSIZE). A malformed LZW stream could provide a code value >= CSIZE, leading to an out-of-bounds read when accessing `codep->firstchar`, `codep->length`, `codep->value`, etc. This could also lead to out-of-bounds writes when the decoded string is written to the output buffer. An attacker crafting a malicious TIFF file could exploit this for information disclosure or arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when traversing code table linked list without NULL checks
vulnerabilities.long_description: Multiple locations traverse the `codep->next` linked list without checking for NULL. In the restart residue path, `do { codep = codep->next; } while (--residue > occ)` and subsequent loops assume the linked list has sufficient length. In the main decode path when `codep->length > occ`, the code does `do { codep = codep->next; } while (codep->length > occ)` which dereferences codep without a NULL check. If the code table is corrupted or the `length` field is inconsistent with the actual linked list depth, a NULL pointer dereference or access through a corrupted pointer will occur, causing a crash or potentially exploitable condition.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer truncation from tmsize_t to long on platforms where they differ in size
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `occ0` of type `tmsize_t` (typically 64-bit on 64-bit platforms) is cast to `long`. On Windows 64-bit, `long` is 32-bit while `tmsize_t` is 64-bit. While there is a check `(tmsize_t) occ != occ0`, a negative `occ0` value that fits in a long would pass this check but cause incorrect behavior since `occ` is used as a signed length. Additionally, all subsequent arithmetic on `occ` uses `long` which could lead to subtle overflow issues on LLP64 platforms.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Failure to abort when setuid/setgid fails allows continued execution with elevated privileges
vulnerabilities.long_description: When setresuid/setuid or setresgid/setgid fails, the function logs an error but continues execution (unless EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is defined and errno is EAGAIN, a very narrow condition). After the failed calls, the function unconditionally updates current_user.euid and current_user.egid to the unprivileged values, making the rest of the program believe privileges were successfully dropped when they were not. This means the process continues running with elevated (e.g., root) privileges while the application's internal bookkeeping claims otherwise. An attacker who can trigger a setuid failure (e.g., via resource limits causing EAGAIN on Linux) could exploit this to perform privileged operations that should have been denied. The setgid failure path has no exit path at all, compounding the issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Internal privilege state updated unconditionally regardless of whether privilege drop succeeded
vulnerabilities.long_description: The assignments to current_user.euid and current_user.egid are executed regardless of whether the preceding setuid/setgid calls succeeded. This creates an inconsistency between the actual OS-level privileges of the process and the application's internal tracking. Any subsequent authorization checks relying on current_user.euid or current_user.egid will incorrectly conclude the process is running unprivileged, potentially allowing privileged operations (file access, command execution) that should be blocked.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in LCM step calculation loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables hsteplcm and vsteplcm are multiplied in a loop by each component's step value without any overflow check. Since both are uint_fast32_t, repeated multiplication can silently overflow/wrap, leading to incorrect values used later in offset calculations (cp->imgareatlx *= hsteplcm). This can cause downstream incorrect memory allocation sizes or incorrect tile/grid computations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image area and reference grid dimension calculations
vulnerabilities.long_description: Several arithmetic operations on uint_fast32_t values can overflow: the multiplication of imgareatlx/imgareatly by hsteplcm/vsteplcm, and the subsequent additions to compute refgrdwidth/refgrdheight. Overflowed values propagate into tile count calculations and memory allocations, potentially enabling heap buffer overflows or other memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile count multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of numhtiles and numvtiles to compute numtiles can overflow. If the resulting numtiles value wraps to a small number, subsequent allocations based on numtiles will be undersized while actual processing may iterate over the expected (larger) number of tiles, leading to heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile grid offset validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The additions cp->tilegrdoffx + cp->tilewidth and cp->tilegrdoffy + cp->tileheight can overflow for large unsigned values, causing the validation check to pass when it should fail. This bypasses an important safety check and allows invalid tile grid parameters to be used in subsequent computations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of atoi for parsing user-controlled input into unsigned variables
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses atoi() to parse user-supplied option strings. atoi() does not detect overflow or invalid input and has undefined behavior for values outside int range. Negative return values from atoi are implicitly converted to large unsigned values when assigned to uint_fast32_t/uint_fast16_t fields (e.g., imgareatlx, tilewidth, jp2overhead), bypassing subsequent validation checks and potentially causing integer overflows in arithmetic operations.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference when accessing dec->cdef without null check in CMAP processing block
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a CMAP box is present (dec->cmap is non-null), the code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef to assign cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef. However, there is no check that dec->cdef is non-null at this point. A crafted JP2 file with a CMAP box but no CDEF box will cause a null pointer dereference, leading to a crash (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference due to missing allocation failure check on lutents
vulnerabilities.long_description: The result of jas_alloc2() for lutents is never checked for NULL before being dereferenced in the subsequent loop. If the allocation fails (e.g., due to memory exhaustion or a very large numlutents value from a crafted file), the code will dereference a null pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on CDEF entries array when numchans exceeds CDEF entry count
vulnerabilities.long_description: The CDEF processing loop iterates up to dec->numchans and accesses dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents[i]. However, the number of entries in the CDEF box (dec->cdef->data.cdef.numchans) may be smaller than dec->numchans (which comes from the CMAP box or image component count). A crafted JP2 file where the CDEF box has fewer entries than dec->numchans would cause an out-of-bounds read on the ents array, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in attribute length leads to heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `attr->hdr.length` (parsed from the network buffer) is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` will underflow, producing an extremely large value for `value.slen`. This large length is then passed to `pj_strdup`, which will attempt to copy a massive amount of memory from the buffer, causing a heap buffer over-read. An attacker can craft a malicious STUN message with a small length field in the ERROR-CODE attribute to trigger this, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0 ; i < len; i+=2) name[i/2] = name[i];
  name[i/2]='\0';
vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when len is 1
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `len` is 1, `cli_malloc(1)` allocates a 1-byte buffer. The loop runs once (i=0, writing name[0]=name[0]), then i becomes 2. After the loop, the null terminator write `name[i/2] = name[2/2] = name[1] = '\0'` writes one byte past the end of the allocated buffer. This is a heap buffer overflow that could potentially be exploited to corrupt heap metadata or adjacent heap objects, leading to code execution. An attacker who can control the `len` parameter (e.g., via a crafted SIS file with a string length field of 1) can trigger this condition.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on data->block[0] in SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write and SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL leads to buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write and I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL cases, data->block[0] (which specifies the block length) is used directly without validating it against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (32). Since data->block[0] is a u8 and can be up to 255, the memcpy operations can write far beyond the bounds of dma_buffer (which is derived from priv->buffer). This contrasts with the I2C_SMBUS_I2C_BLOCK_DATA case which properly clamps data->block[0] to [1, I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX]. Additionally, in the SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, dma_size is a u8 computed as data->block[0] + 1, which wraps to 0 when data->block[0] is 255, causing further inconsistencies between the DMA mapping size and the actual bytes written. An attacker who can trigger an SMBus transaction with a crafted block length could corrupt kernel heap memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow of u8 dma_size when data->block[0] is 255 in SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable dma_size is declared as u8. In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write path, dma_size is assigned data->block[0] + 1. If data->block[0] is 255, this overflows to 0. A dma_size of 0 means the DMA mapping is skipped entirely (the 'if (dma_size != 0)' check fails), yet the memcpy with the original unchecked length has already written up to 255 bytes into dma_buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. The descriptor would also have inconsistent state since wr_len_cmd would be 0.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of BINDING struct when integer overflow checks fail after its allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the else branch (new BINDING allocation), if `b` is successfully allocated via MALLOC but one of the subsequent integer overflow checks for `len + EXPAND_SPARE` triggers, the function returns XML_ERROR_NO_MEMORY without freeing `b`. This contrasts with the later check where `b->uri` allocation fails and `FREE(parser, b)` is correctly called. An attacker who can control the URI length to be close to INT_MAX could trigger these overflow checks repeatedly, causing a denial-of-service through memory exhaustion.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size*count before cast to long long leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `size*count + pad+20` is computed using uint32_t arithmetic before being cast to `long long`. If `size` (up to 8) and `count` (attacker-controlled uint32_t) multiply to a value exceeding UINT32_MAX, the result wraps around to a small value. This small value passes the `allocate > io.size()` check, causing a tiny buffer to be allocated. Subsequently, `io.read(buf.pData_, count*size)` uses the same overflowed uint32_t product, potentially writing far more data than the buffer can hold, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a malicious image with a large `count` field to trigger this, leading to arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded recursive calls with attacker-controlled offset can cause stack overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `option == kpsRecursive`, the function calls itself recursively using `offset` values read from the file. A crafted file can create circular IFD references (e.g., an IFD entry pointing back to a previous IFD), causing infinite recursion. The `depth` variable is incremented but never checked against a maximum limit. This leads to stack exhaustion and a denial-of-service crash.

code_snippets:
for ( size_t k = 0 ; k < kount ; k++ ) {
                            out << sp << byteSwap2(buf,k*size,bSwap);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing buf with kount derived from count but buf may be undersized due to integer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When the integer overflow in `size*count` produces a small allocation, the loops that iterate up to `kount` (which can equal `count` for XMP/ICC tags, or up to 5/32 otherwise) access `buf` at offsets `k*size` via byteSwap functions. Since the buffer is undersized due to the overflow, these reads go beyond the allocated buffer, leaking heap memory contents. This can be exploited for information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when null-terminating XMP buffer after integer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: For XMP tags, after the buffer is allocated with the potentially overflowed size, `buf.pData_[count]=0` writes a null byte at offset `count` into the buffer. If the buffer was allocated with a much smaller size due to integer overflow in `size*count`, this write occurs far beyond the buffer boundary, corrupting heap metadata or other data. This can be exploited for arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * nmemb multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of size and nmemb is performed without checking for integer overflow. If an attacker can influence these values (e.g., via a malicious HTTP server response), the result could wrap around, producing a small value for 'bytes' while the actual data in 'ptr' is much larger. Although 'bytes' is only used as the return value here, the real concern is that the buffer 'ptr' may not be treated with correct bounds throughout the function, and the overflow could mask the true size of data being processed.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that ptr buffer length is sufficient before reading at fixed offsets
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses strncasecmp and then accesses ptr at offsets like url->auth_header_len, url->timelimit_header_len, and 22 without verifying that 'bytes' (the actual data length) is large enough. For example, if bytes < url->timelimit_header_len, the sscanf call reads past the buffer. Similarly, if bytes < 22, ptr+22 reads out of bounds. The ptr buffer from libcurl is not guaranteed to be null-terminated, so sscanf and snprintf with '%s' could read beyond the allocated buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads and potential information disclosure or crashes.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed integer overflow when parsing verbose level
vulnerabilities.long_description: The verbose level is parsed using atoi(), which can return values up to INT_MAX. Adding 1 to the result causes a signed integer overflow when the input value is INT_MAX (e.g., the string "2147483647" on 32-bit int systems). Signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C, and depending on the implementation, cmod_verbose could wrap to INT_MIN or another unexpected value. This could lead to incorrect verbose behavior, bypass of verbose-level checks, or other unpredictable consequences in code that later compares cmod_verbose against expected threshold values.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer underflow in entry_length calculation when offsets are not properly ordered
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `idxt.offsets[curr_number + 1]` is smaller than `idxt.offsets[curr_number]`, the subtraction of unsigned `size_t` values wraps around to a very large number. While a subsequent check `buf->offset + entry_length >= buf_maxlen` exists, the addition `buf->offset + entry_length` could itself overflow, potentially bypassing the guard and leading to out-of-bounds reads later when the buffer's maxlen is set based on this corrupted value.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on control_bytes pointer without bounds validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The `control_bytes` pointer is set to the current buffer position, then `mobi_buffer_seek` advances past `tagx->control_byte_count` bytes. However, there is no validation that `control_byte_count` bytes actually remain in the buffer, nor that subsequent `control_bytes++` increments (when `tagx->tags[i].control_byte == 1`) keep the pointer within the allocated buffer. An attacker crafting a malicious file with a large `control_byte_count` or many control-byte-tagged entries could cause reads beyond the buffer boundary.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Buffer maxlen not restored on early error return paths, leaving buffer in corrupt state
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function saves `buf->maxlen` and then narrows it. Multiple error return paths (e.g., `label_length > entry_length`, malloc failures for tags) exit without restoring `buf->maxlen` to `buf_maxlen`. This leaves the buffer object in an inconsistent state for subsequent callers, potentially enabling out-of-bounds access or denial of service in later buffer operations.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in count computation may lead to excessive loop iterations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `ptagx[i].value_count * ptagx[i].tag_value_count` is performed with uint32_t arithmetic (value_count is uint32_t). If an attacker controls these values through a crafted file, the product can overflow, potentially resulting in a very large or very small `count`. Although the loop is bounded by `INDX_TAGVALUES_MAX`, repeated calls to `mobi_buffer_get_varlen` with a corrupted count could still read beyond the buffer if `mobi_buffer_get_varlen` does not properly check bounds.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write to prcwidthexpns/prcheightexpns arrays via user-controlled maxrlvls before bounds validation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The user can set tccp->maxrlvls to an arbitrarily large value via the OPT_MAXRLVLS option (parsed from atoi). The loop that writes to tccp->prcwidthexpns[rlvlno] and tccp->prcheightexpns[rlvlno] uses this value as the upper bound, but the validation check (maxrlvls < 1) occurs much later in the function. If maxrlvls exceeds the size of the statically-allocated prcwidthexpns/prcheightexpns arrays, this results in a heap or stack buffer overflow, potentially enabling code execution.

code_snippets:
hsteplcm *= jas_image_cmpthstep(image, cmptno);
vsteplcm *= jas_image_cmptvstep(image, cmptno);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in LCM accumulation of component step sizes
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables hsteplcm and vsteplcm are multiplied by each component's step size in a loop without any overflow checking. With multiple components having large step sizes, these uint_fast32_t values can overflow silently, leading to incorrect (smaller) values. These are later used in multiplication with cp->imgareatlx and cp->imgareatly, which can cascade into further incorrect calculations for reference grid dimensions and tile parameters.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in tile count multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of cp->numhtiles and cp->numvtiles to compute cp->numtiles can overflow, resulting in an incorrectly small value. This value is later used for memory allocation and loop bounds elsewhere in the encoder. An attacker can craft tile dimensions that cause a large number of horizontal and vertical tiles whose product overflows, leading to undersized allocations and subsequent heap buffer overflows.

code_snippets:
cp->refgrdwidth = cp->imgareatlx + jas_image_width(image);
cp->refgrdheight = cp->imgareatly + jas_image_height(image);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in reference grid dimension and image area offset calculations
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of cp->imgareatlx by hsteplcm and subsequent addition with image width can overflow. Similarly for the y-axis. These overflowed values are then used in tile grid offset validation checks (which themselves can overflow, e.g., cp->tilegrdoffx + cp->tilewidth), making the validation ineffective. This can lead to inconsistent internal state and potential memory corruption downstream.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.

commit_message: Bug 705156(1): Avoid double free of PCL error buffer
cve_desc: A vulnerability classified as problematic was found in GhostPCL 9.55.0. This vulnerability affects the function chunk_free_object of the file gsmchunk.c. The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unsigned integer underflow when read_pos exceeds write_pos leads to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: If `pcs->status.read_pos` is ever greater than `pcs->status.write_pos`, the subtraction `write_pos - read_pos` underflows because both are unsigned integers, wrapping around to a very large value. The `min` with `max_data` would then select `max_data`, and the subsequent `memcpy` would read up to `max_data` bytes from a potentially invalid offset in the buffer. This constitutes a heap-based out-of-bounds read that could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash. An attacker who can influence the state of `read_pos` or `write_pos` (e.g., through malformed input or concurrent access) could exploit this to read arbitrary heap data.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on cp950_pua_tbl when no matching entry is found
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the for-loop completes without finding a matching entry (i.e., `c2` does not fall within any range in `cp950_pua_tbl`), `k` will equal the table size. The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k]` without checking whether `k` is still within bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds read. This could cause a crash or leak sensitive memory contents, and an attacker who controls the input byte sequence could trigger this reliably.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on data->block[0] in I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write path leads to buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, data->block[0] is used without validation against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (32). Since dma_size is a u8, if data->block[0] is 255, then data->block[0] + 1 overflows to 0, and the subsequent memcpy with dma_size - 1 (which wraps to 255 as an unsigned value) copies up to 255 bytes into dma_buffer, overflowing the priv->buffer. Compare with the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case which correctly checks data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX before proceeding. This can corrupt adjacent memory in the priv structure, potentially leading to code execution or denial of service.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read on cp950_pua_tbl when loop completes without finding a match
vulnerabilities.long_description: If the for-loop iterating over cp950_pua_tbl completes without breaking (i.e., no entry matches c2), k will equal the table size. The code then accesses cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] and other elements without checking whether k is still within bounds. This results in an out-of-bounds read, which could leak sensitive memory contents or cause a crash. Although is_in_cp950_pua() is called as a guard, if its logic is inconsistent with the table contents, the OOB access will occur.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use-after-free: accessing nfc_dev after nci_unregister_device may have freed it
vulnerabilities.long_description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called first, which internally calls nfc_unregister_device() and can free the underlying nfc_dev structure. Immediately after, the code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, accessing memory that may have already been freed. An attacker or specific timing condition could cause this freed memory to be reallocated with attacker-controlled data, leading to incorrect control flow (e.g., skipping or entering the fw_download_in_progress branch) or a kernel crash. The check for fw_download_in_progress and the potential call to nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort should occur before nci_unregister_device.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in allocation size calculation (elem_size * max_entries)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` can overflow a u64. `elem_size` can be up to approximately sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + U32_MAX (since value_size is a u32), and `max_entries` is also a u32 (up to U32_MAX). Their product can exceed 2^64 and wrap around, resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. The subsequent `pcpu_freelist_populate` call then initializes `max_entries` elements of `elem_size` bytes each within this undersized buffer, causing massive out-of-bounds writes. An attacker who can control `value_size` and `max_entries` (e.g., via BPF map creation syscall) could exploit this for heap corruption, potentially leading to privilege escalation.

commit_message: Fix ultra rare but potential memory-leak
cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in bytes_per_line and length calculations leading to heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The calculation of `bytes_per_line` involves multiplying `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel`, which can overflow on 32-bit systems (or even 64-bit with extreme values). If this overflows, `bytes_per_line` becomes smaller than expected, and consequently `length` (bytes_per_line * image->rows) also wraps. The subsequent blob size check divides `length` by 8, making it even weaker and allowing crafted files to pass validation. The pixel buffer is then allocated with potentially incorrect size, and during pixel processing loops that compute `p=pixels+(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line`, out-of-bounds reads from heap memory can occur when the actual row stride expected by the loop logic exceeds the allocated buffer size.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in image->columns * image->rows passed to DecodeImage for RLE decompression
vulnerabilities.long_description: When handling RLE-compressed BMP images, the product `image->columns * image->rows` is passed as a size parameter to `DecodeImage`. This multiplication can overflow for large image dimensions, resulting in a much smaller value being passed. This could cause DecodeImage to write decoded pixel data beyond the intended bounds of the allocated buffer, or process data incorrectly, potentially leading to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft a BMP file with large width and height values along with RLE compression to trigger this overflow.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Insufficient image data validation due to dividing length by 8 in size check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The check intended to validate that the file contains enough data divides the expected `length` by 8 before comparing against the blob size. This means a crafted BMP file only needs to be 1/8th the size of the expected pixel data to pass this validation. While the subsequent `ReadBlob` count check catches reads of uncompressed data, for RLE-compressed paths (which don't use this count check), the weak validation could allow processing to proceed with insufficient data, leading to reads of uninitialized heap memory or other undefined behavior.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow when shifting 1 by subsystem index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `(1 << i)` uses a signed 32-bit integer literal `1`. If the subsystem index `i` is 31 or greater, this results in undefined behavior (shifting into or past the sign bit of a signed integer). Even for values less than 31 but greater than the width of `subsys_mask`, bits could be silently lost. This could allow an attacker to bypass intended subsystem restrictions by specifying a controller whose index triggers the overflow, potentially enabling unauthorized access to cgroup subsystems. The fix is to use `(1U << i)` or `(1UL << i)` to ensure unsigned shifting with sufficient width.

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' '%s' '%s' '%s'",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(title,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(description,"'", "%27"),
		                                       utils::replace_all(feed_title,"'", "%27"));
vulnerabilities.short_description: OS command injection via incomplete shell metacharacter sanitization
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code attempts to prevent shell injection by replacing single quotes with %27, then wrapping each argument in single quotes. However, this is insufficient. An attacker who controls the url, title, description, or feed_title (e.g., via a malicious RSS feed) can inject shell commands using other techniques. For instance, a value containing a literal backslash followed by a single quote, or depending on shell behavior, newline characters or other control characters could break out of the quoting context. More critically, if any input contains characters like $(command) or `command` these would not be escaped — while they are normally inert inside single quotes, any edge case where the quoting is broken (e.g., via embedded null bytes truncating the string in C) could lead to arbitrary command execution. The command string is passed to /bin/sh -c, meaning any successful injection runs with the privileges of the application.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `len` is computed as `(int)(p - *arg + extra)`, where `p - *arg` is a `ptrdiff_t`. If the input string is very large (e.g., approaching or exceeding 2GB), truncating the result of the pointer difference to `int` causes integer overflow / undefined behavior. This could result in `len` becoming a small positive number, causing `alloc()` to allocate a tiny buffer while the subsequent copy loop writes a much larger amount of data, producing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker who controls the input string could exploit this for arbitrary code execution.

code_snippets: extra = trans_special(&p, end, flags, FALSE, NULL);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Bounds check after trans_special is performed after the write, not preventing the overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The call to `trans_special(&p, end, ...)` writes data directly into the buffer at `end`. The bounds check `if (end >= rettv->vval.v_string + len)` occurs only after `trans_special` has already written past the buffer boundary. If the first-pass size estimation is wrong (e.g., `find_special_key` and `trans_special` disagree on the interpretation of a sequence), the overflow has already corrupted heap memory by the time the check fires. Furthermore, the check only emits a warning via `iemsg()` and does not abort, so execution continues with a corrupted heap.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * nmemb multiplication
vulnerabilities.long_description: The multiplication of `size` and `nmemb` (both `size_t`) is performed without any overflow check. If a malicious or misbehaving source provides values where `size * nmemb` wraps around, `len` could become a very small value. This small `len` would then be used in subsequent length comparisons (e.g., `len >= url->auth_header_len`, `len > 24`), potentially allowing the `strncasecmp` or `memcpy` operations to read or write out of bounds relative to the actual data in `ptr`. This is the classic libcurl write-callback integer overflow pattern.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Null pointer dereference due to missing null checks on client->auth and auth->state
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `client` is non-null, the code unconditionally dereferences `client->auth` and then `auth->state` without checking if either is NULL. If `client->auth` is NULL (e.g., during a race condition or unexpected state), dereferencing it to access `state` will cause a null pointer dereference, leading to a crash (denial of service).

commit_message:
cgroup-v1: Require capabilities to set release_agent

The cgroup release_agent is called with call_usermodehelper.  The function
call_usermodehelper starts the release_agent with a full set fo capabilities.
Therefore require capabilities when setting the release_agaent.

Reported-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Fixes: 81a6a5cdd2c5 ("Task Control Groups: automatic userspace notification of idle cgroups")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v2.6.24+
Signed-off-by: "Eric W. Biederman" <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel’s cgroup_release_agent_write in the kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c function. This flaw, under certain circumstances, allows the use of the cgroups v1 release_agent feature to escalate privileges and bypass the namespace isolation unexpectedly.

commit_message:
SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards

(cherry picked from commit f3604b901d688c194de5e430c7fbab060c9dc8e0)
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

Based on a reported DoS vulnerability reported by Team82 (Claroty
Research).

An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending an unlimited number of huge chunks (e.g. 2GB each) without sending the Final closing chunk.
