commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message:
MDEV-25787 Bug report: crash on SELECT DISTINCT thousands_blob_fields

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash via certain long SELECT DISTINCT statements that improperly interact with storage-engine resource limitations for temporary data structures.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

code_snippets:
data->pos_delta += - counted * deltanext +
			(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount - minnext * mincount;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in regex quantifier length calculations
vulnerabilities.long_description: Several arithmetic operations involving minnext, mincount, maxcount, deltanext, and related variables can overflow. While there is an overflow check before `min += minnext * mincount`, the subsequent calculations for `data->pos_min` and `data->pos_delta` lack similar overflow guards. The expression `(minnext + deltanext) * maxcount` can overflow when maxcount is large (e.g. REG_INFTY) and minnext + deltanext is non-trivial. Similarly, `minnext * (mincount - counted)` added to `data->pos_min` lacks overflow protection. These overflows could lead to incorrect length calculations, potentially causing buffer over-reads or over-writes during regex matching.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SvGROW size calculation for repeated string
vulnerabilities.long_description: When mincount and l (string length) are both large values, the expression `mincount * l` can overflow, causing SvGROW to allocate a much smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent repeatcpy then writes past the allocated buffer, leading to a heap buffer overflow. This could be triggered by crafting a regex with a large quantifier on a constant string pattern, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when storing literal byte without checking window_posn against window_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the literal byte path (selector < 4), the code writes `window[window_posn++] = sym` without verifying that `window_posn` is within the bounds of the allocated window buffer (`qtm->window_size`). The `frame_end` calculation is derived from `out_bytes` and may exceed the window size. An attacker crafting a malicious compressed stream could cause writes past the end of the window buffer, leading to heap corruption and potential code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write during match copy when window_posn + match_length exceeds window_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the match-copy paths (selectors 4, 5, 6), when `match_offset > window_posn` (the wrap case), the code copies `match_length` bytes to `&window[window_posn]` without checking whether `window_posn + match_length` exceeds `qtm->window_size`. This allows writing beyond the allocated window buffer. In the non-wrap else branch, the copy length `i` is clamped but `window_posn += match_length` still uses the unclamped value, causing window_posn to exceed window_size and enabling subsequent out-of-bounds accesses. A crafted compressed stream can trigger heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
i = qtm->o_end - qtm->o_ptr;
  if ((off_t) i > out_bytes) i = (int) out_bytes;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer truncation when casting pointer difference and out_bytes to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The difference `qtm->o_end - qtm->o_ptr` is stored in an `int`, and `out_bytes` (an `off_t`, typically 64-bit) is cast to `int` for comparison and assignment. If `out_bytes` or the pointer difference is larger than INT_MAX, this results in integer truncation/overflow, which could lead to incorrect length values being passed to `mspack_write`, potentially causing under-reads or other memory safety issues.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Algorithm name copied from network data without ensuring null termination
vulnerabilities.long_description: The alg_name field is copied directly from attacker-controlled network message data via memcpy without ensuring it is null-terminated. If the received data contains TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes with no null byte, any subsequent use of skey->alg_name as a C string (e.g., when passed to the kernel crypto API's crypto_alloc_aead or similar functions, or in logging) will read beyond the bounds of the buffer. This can lead to information disclosure or potentially a crash. An attacker can craft a malicious TIPC message with a non-null-terminated algorithm name to trigger this.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded prefix-table traversal can cause stack buffer underflow via crafted input
vulnerabilities.long_description: The while loop that traces back through the LZW prefix table has no bounds check on how far `stackp` can decrement, nor does it detect cycles in the prefix table. A maliciously crafted compressed stream can create a cycle in `tab_prefixof` entries (e.g., code A points to code B which points back to A). This would cause the loop to run indefinitely, decrementing `stackp` past the beginning of `de_stack`, resulting in an out-of-bounds write. Even without a cycle, a very long chain could exhaust the `de_stack` buffer. This can lead to memory corruption, denial of service, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Write to outbuf without bounds check when processing first code
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `oldcode == -1` (first code processing), the code writes to `outbuf[outpos++]` without verifying that `outpos` is less than `OUTBUFSIZ`. While this is typically only hit once at the start, if a crafted input contains repeated CLEAR codes followed by data codes, this path can be reached multiple times without the output buffer ever being flushed, potentially writing beyond the bounds of `outbuf`.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one out-of-bounds read when p equals end
vulnerabilities.long_description: When *data equals end, the check `p > end` is false (since p == end), so execution proceeds to `len = *p++`, which dereferences `p` at the `end` position. Since `end` typically points one past the last valid byte of the buffer, this is an out-of-bounds read. An attacker who controls the input data length could trigger this to read one byte beyond the allocated buffer, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash. The fix is to change the check to `p >= end`.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference when find_next_block() returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function calls find_next_block() in multiple places (both in the main body and inside the COPY_BUF macro) without ever checking if the return value is NULL. If find_next_block() returns NULL (e.g., due to a truncated or malformed archive), the code immediately dereferences it via b->buffer or blk->buffer, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. An attacker can craft a malicious sparse tar archive that triggers this condition, causing a crash (denial of service) or potentially exploitable behavior depending on the platform.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Attacker-controlled allocation size can cause excessive memory consumption
vulnerabilities.long_description: The sparse_map_size value is read directly from the archive data and bounded only by TYPE_MAXIMUM(size_t). This attacker-controlled value is passed directly to xcalloc, allowing an attacker to craft an archive that requests an extremely large memory allocation (up to SIZE_MAX * sizeof(sp_array)), potentially exhausting system memory and causing denial of service. While xcalloc internally checks for multiplication overflow, the resulting allocation size can still be enormous.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Off-by-one error allows out-of-bounds read when p equals end
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check uses `p > end` instead of `p >= end`. When `p` is exactly equal to `end`, the check passes, and the subsequent `*p++` dereferences memory at the `end` pointer, which is typically one past the last valid byte of the buffer. This is a classic off-by-one out-of-bounds read that could leak sensitive data or cause a crash. An attacker who controls the input data positioning could trigger this condition to read one byte beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in SvGROW size calculation leads to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: When processing a CURLY quantifier with a constant string body, the code computes `(mincount * l) + 1` for SvGROW where `mincount` is an SSize_t and `l` is a STRLEN. If `mincount * l` overflows, a much smaller buffer than needed is allocated. Subsequently, `repeatcpy` copies `l * (mincount - 1)` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. The earlier overflow check uses `minnext` (the recursive study_chunk result) rather than `l` (the actual byte length of the matched string from `SvPV_const`), so these are different values and the check does not protect this code path. A crafted regular expression with a large quantifier on a sufficiently long exact string could trigger this to corrupt heap memory, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if alloc_root returns NULL
vulnerabilities.long_description: If alloc_root fails and returns NULL, the subsequent memcpy and strcpy operations will dereference the NULL pointer, leading to a crash. An attacker who can trigger memory exhaustion could exploit this to cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in size argument to uni_utf8_get_char_n causes potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The size calculation `(size_t)(p - parser->end)` is backwards. Since `p < parser->end` (as guaranteed by the while-loop condition), `p - parser->end` is a negative value. When cast to `size_t` (an unsigned type), this wraps around to a very large positive value (e.g., near SIZE_MAX). This tells `uni_utf8_get_char_n` that an enormous buffer is available, allowing it to read well beyond the actual end of the buffer. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`. An attacker supplying crafted input could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when decoding literal bytes without checking window_size
vulnerabilities.long_description: When `selector < 4` (literal byte path), `window[window_posn++] = sym` writes to the window without any bounds check against `qtm->window_size`. The `frame_end` is calculated based on `out_bytes` and frame size, but is never clamped to `window_size`. While the match-copy path (selectors 4-6) explicitly checks `window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size`, the literal path lacks this check. If `frame_end` exceeds `window_size`, `window_posn` can increment past the allocated window buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker crafting a malicious compressed stream could exploit this to write arbitrary bytes beyond the window buffer.

code_snippets:
i = qtm->o_end - qtm->o_ptr;
  if ((off_t) i > out_bytes) i = (int) out_bytes;
vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer truncation when casting off_t out_bytes to int
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variable `out_bytes` is of type `off_t` (typically 64-bit on modern systems), but it is cast to `int` (typically 32-bit) in multiple places: once near the start (`i = (int) out_bytes`) and once near the end (`i = (int) out_bytes`). If `out_bytes` exceeds INT_MAX, the cast silently truncates the value, potentially resulting in a small or negative value for `i`. This could lead to incorrect write lengths passed to `mspack_write`, potentially causing under-writes or, if negative and interpreted as a large unsigned value, buffer over-reads.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential unsigned integer overflow in window bounds check
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `window_posn + match_length` could potentially overflow if `match_length` is large enough (derived from attacker-controlled compressed data via `qtm->length_base[sym] + extra + 5`). If `window_posn` is near UINT_MAX and `match_length` causes a wraparound, the sum could be smaller than `qtm->window_size`, bypassing the bounds check and leading to an out-of-bounds write via the subsequent copy loop. The `match_length` value is partially controlled by the compressed input through symbol decoding and extra bits.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in size calculation passed to uni_utf8_get_char_n leads to potential out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The length argument is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the subtraction `p - parser->end` yields a negative value. When cast to `size_t` (an unsigned type), this wraps around to a very large positive number. This tells `uni_utf8_get_char_n` that there is far more data available than actually exists, which could cause it to read past the end of the buffer. The correct expression should be `(size_t)(parser->end - p)`. An attacker could craft input that triggers an out-of-bounds memory read, potentially leaking sensitive information or causing a crash.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in width * height calculation leads to undersized heap buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The width and height values are read from the untrusted FLX file header. Their multiplication as guint can overflow, resulting in a small value for flxdec->size. Subsequent g_malloc calls allocate undersized buffers, but later operations (flx_decode_chunks, memcpy with flxdec->size, colorspace conversion) may write data based on the actual width/height dimensions, causing heap buffer overflows. An attacker can craft a FLX file with large width/height values to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * 4 leads to undersized output buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: Even if flxdec->size doesn't overflow from width*height, multiplying it by 4 for the RGBA output buffer can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in an undersized buffer. The subsequent flx_colorspace_convert call writes width*height*4 bytes into this buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can craft width/height values such that width*height fits in a guint but width*height*4 does not.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when flxfh.size is less than FlxFrameChunkSize
vulnerabilities.long_description: The frame chunk size (flxfh.size) is read from the untrusted input. If flxfh.size is non-zero but less than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction `flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize` underflows (as an unsigned value), leading to an extremely large allocation request or reading far beyond available adapter data. There is no check that flxfh.size >= FlxFrameChunkSize before this subtraction.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of chunk buffer and output buffer on decode error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When flx_decode_chunks fails, the function returns GST_FLOW_ERROR immediately without freeing 'chunk' (allocated via gst_adapter_take) or unreferencing the 'out' GstBuffer (allocated via gst_buffer_new_and_alloc). This causes memory leaks that can be triggered repeatedly by an attacker providing malformed FLX frames, potentially leading to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in width * height calculation leads to undersized heap allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: The width and height fields are read from the untrusted FLX file header. Their multiplication can overflow a guint (32-bit), resulting in a much smaller value stored in flxdec->size. Subsequent g_malloc calls allocate undersized buffers, while later operations (memcpy, flx_decode_chunks, flx_colorspace_convert) operate on the full logical frame size, causing heap buffer overflows. An attacker can craft a FLX file with large width/height values to trigger this.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in size * 4 can cause undersized output buffer allocation
vulnerabilities.long_description: Even if flxdec->size does not overflow during width*height, multiplying it by 4 for the RGBA output buffer can overflow a 32-bit integer. This results in an undersized buffer, while flx_colorspace_convert writes width*height*4 bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can set width and height such that their product fits in 32 bits but the product times 4 does not.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow when flxfh.size < FlxFrameChunkSize leads to huge allocation/read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The flxfh.size value is read from untrusted input. If flxfh.size is smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction underflows (both are unsigned), resulting in a very large value passed to gst_adapter_take. This can cause out-of-bounds reads or denial of service. The check 'avail < flxfh.size' does not prevent flxfh.size from being smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak of 'chunk' and 'out' buffer on decode error path
vulnerabilities.long_description: When flx_decode_chunks fails, the function returns GST_FLOW_ERROR immediately without freeing 'chunk' (allocated via gst_adapter_take) or unreffing 'out' (allocated via gst_buffer_new_and_alloc). This causes memory leaks that could be triggered repeatedly by a malicious file, leading to resource exhaustion.

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Stored/Reflected XSS via unescaped log file content rendered in HTML
vulnerabilities.long_description: The contents of the log file are read and inserted directly into an HTML textarea without any HTML entity encoding. If an attacker can influence log file contents (e.g., by triggering log entries containing malicious strings like '</textarea><script>…</script>'), the injected HTML/JavaScript will be rendered in the browser of any user viewing the log page. This allows cross-site scripting attacks that could steal session cookies or perform actions on behalf of the authenticated user.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when substituting macro arguments into tmpFile.line without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding macro arguments into tmpFile.line, the code copies characters from the argument without checking if tmpFile.linelen exceeds MAXSTRING (or the bounds of tmpFile.line). While individual definition characters are checked against MAXSTRING, the argument substitution loop has no such check. A crafted macro with long arguments can overflow the tmpFile.line buffer, leading to arbitrary memory corruption. This could be exploited to achieve code execution by carefully controlling the contents of macro arguments.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak: allocated 'arguments' array is never freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'arguments' array is allocated with malloc() during macro expansion but is never freed on any code path—neither on the successful return (return 1) nor on several error paths (e.g., when compileRule fails or when the argument count check fails). Repeated invocations of macro expansion will leak memory proportional to the number of macro arguments, which could lead to resource exhaustion (denial of service) when processing many macros.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on arguments array via unchecked substitution index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The index used to access the 'arguments' array is derived from m->substitutions[2 * subst + 1] - 1, which comes from the macro definition. If this value is 0 (yielding index -1) or greater than m->argument_count, it results in an out-of-bounds read on the heap-allocated arguments array. A maliciously crafted macro definition could specify substitution indices outside the valid range, leading to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap buffer overflow when substituting macro arguments into tmpFile.line without bounds checking
vulnerabilities.long_description: When expanding a macro, individual definition characters are checked against MAXSTRING before writing to tmpFile.line. However, when substituting macro arguments, the entire argument's characters are copied into tmpFile.line in a loop without any bounds check on tmpFile.linelen. If the combined length of the macro definition and substituted arguments exceeds MAXSTRING, this writes past the end of the tmpFile.line buffer. An attacker who can control the content of a translation table file could craft a macro invocation with long arguments to trigger a heap/stack buffer overflow, potentially leading to code execution.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak: allocated 'arguments' array is never freed
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the macro expansion code path, memory is allocated for the 'arguments' array via malloc(m->argument_count * sizeof(CharsString)). On the success path (return 1) and on some error paths (e.g., when compileRule recursion fails), this allocation is never freed. Each macro invocation during table compilation leaks this memory. While not directly exploitable for code execution, a malicious table file with many macro calls could cause denial of service through memory exhaustion.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential out-of-bounds read on arguments array via unvalidated substitution index
vulnerabilities.long_description: The index used to access the 'arguments' array is derived from m->substitutions[2 * subst + 1] - 1. There is no validation that this index is within the range [0, m->argument_count). If the macro's substitution data contains an index that is 0 (resulting in -1 after subtraction) or greater than argument_count, this leads to an out-of-bounds read on the 'arguments' array. Depending on the memory layout, this could leak sensitive data or cause a crash.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len
vulnerabilities.long_description: If dl->length is less than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression `dl->length - so_ext_len` will underflow because both operands are of type size_t (unsigned). This results in a very large pointer offset, causing my_strcasecmp to read from an out-of-bounds memory location. An attacker providing a very short dynamic library name could trigger this to read arbitrary memory or crash the server.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.short_description: NULL pointer dereference if alloc_root fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: The return value of alloc_root is not checked for NULL. If the memory allocation fails, the subsequent memcpy and strcpy calls will write to a NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing upper bounds check on source and destination when offset is negative
vulnerabilities.long_description: When offset is negative, the code only verifies that the source pointer does not underflow (buf->offset >= aoffset), but it never checks that the source region (buf->data + buf->offset - aoffset + len) or the destination region (buf->data + buf->offset + len) stays within buf->maxlen. An attacker supplying a negative offset with a large len value can cause memmove to read and write beyond the allocated buffer, leading to heap-based out-of-bounds read and write.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Undefined behavior when offset equals INT_MIN due to abs(INT_MIN)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The C standard states that abs(INT_MIN) is undefined behavior because the result cannot be represented as an int. On most two's complement implementations, abs(INT_MIN) returns INT_MIN (a negative value), which when cast to size_t becomes an extremely large value. This corrupts all subsequent bounds checks and pointer arithmetic, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access. An attacker can trigger this by passing INT_MIN as the offset parameter.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential integer overflow in bounds check when offset is non-negative
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression buf->offset + aoffset + len is computed using size_t arithmetic. If aoffset and len are both large values (which is possible especially given the abs(INT_MIN) issue), this addition can wrap around to a small value, causing the bounds check to pass incorrectly. The subsequent memmove would then operate on out-of-bounds memory.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Zip Slip: path traversal via crafted archive entry filenames
vulnerabilities.long_description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly in getChildFile without validating that the resulting path stays within targetDirectory. A malicious zip entry with a name like "../../etc/cron.d/evil" would cause files to be written outside the intended extraction directory, potentially overwriting arbitrary files on the system.

code_snippets:
if (! File::createSymbolicLink (targetFile, originalFilePath, true))
            return Result::fail ("Failed to create symbolic link: " + originalFilePath);
vulnerabilities.short_description: Arbitrary symlink creation allows writing/reading outside target directory
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a zip entry is marked as a symbolic link, the code creates a symlink pointing to an arbitrary path read from the zip stream. An attacker can craft a zip file that first creates a symlink pointing outside the target directory, and then a subsequent entry following that symlink writes arbitrary content to an attacker-controlled location. There is no validation of the symlink target.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

Problem:    Crash with weird 'vartabstop' value.
Solution:   Check for running into the end of the line.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4436.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential heap buffer overflow when truncating new_line at cursor column in VREPLACE mode
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the VREPLACE block at the end of the function, `new_line` is allocated as a copy of the current line via `vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())`. Its buffer size is `strlen(ml_get_curline()) + 1`. Then `new_line[curwin->w_cursor.col] = NUL` writes at the cursor column offset. However, `curwin->w_cursor.col` was set earlier in the function based on `new_cursor_col`, which is computed through multiple complex code paths (including arithmetic with `i` for inserted spaces: `new_cursor_col += i`). If `curwin->w_cursor.col` ends up exceeding the actual length of the string returned by `ml_get_curline()` at that later point (e.g., if `ins_str` in the space-insertion path failed or the line state changed between the cursor computation and the VREPLACE finalization), the write goes out of bounds of the heap-allocated buffer, leading to heap corruption. An attacker who can control the indent change scenario (e.g., via a crafted file with specific multibyte characters and virtual column settings) could potentially exploit this for code execution.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Inconsistent descriptor size: desc_first read/write uses full DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS size instead of actual descriptor length
vulnerabilities.long_description: When reading and writing back the first descriptor (desc_first) to mark it as used, the code uses sizeof(desc_first) which equals sizeof(uint32_t) * DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS. However, all other descriptor reads in the function correctly use sizeof(uint32_t) * gem_get_desc_len(s, false) to determine the actual descriptor size. If DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS is larger than the value returned by gem_get_desc_len(s, false), the address_space_read and address_space_write calls will access guest DMA memory beyond the actual descriptor boundary. This could corrupt adjacent descriptors or other data structures in guest memory, and the write-back could overwrite neighboring memory with stale/uninitialized data from the stack-allocated desc_first buffer, leading to information disclosure from the host stack into guest DMA memory or corruption of guest state.

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Race condition when modifying a potentially shared SSL_SESSION object (CVE-2015-1791)
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function directly modifies s->session in place (freeing and reallocating tlsext_tick, updating session_id, etc.) without checking whether the session object is shared via the session cache or referenced by other SSL connections. If another thread is concurrently using the same SSL_SESSION (e.g., for resumption), this leads to a race condition that can cause use-after-free or double-free when OPENSSL_free is called on tlsext_tick. The fix requires checking if the session's reference count is greater than 1 and, if so, creating a duplicate/new session before modification. An attacker-controlled server could trigger this by sending a NewSessionTicket message, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service in multi-threaded clients.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unbounded fscanf %s can overflow the stack buffer 'str'
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses fscanf with a bare "%s" format specifier to read into the stack-allocated buffer 'str' (of size GmfStrSiz). Since %s has no width limit, a maliciously crafted mesh file containing a very long token (longer than GmfStrSiz) will overflow the buffer, leading to stack-based buffer overflow. This can be exploited for arbitrary code execution or denial of service. A width specifier should be added, e.g., "%255s" matching GmfStrSiz-1.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory leak when ScaKwdTab fails: msh is not freed before returning 0
vulnerabilities.long_description: When ScaKwdTab(msh) returns 0 (failure), the function returns 0 without freeing the allocated 'msh' structure or closing any open file handles. This leaks the memory allocated by calloc and potentially leaks the open file descriptor/handle. An attacker could trigger this repeatedly with malformed files to exhaust memory.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds read when params->base->length is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code checks that params->base and params->base->data are non-NULL, but does not verify that params->base->length is at least 1 before accessing params->base->data[0]. If a crafted ECPARAMETERS structure provides a base point octet string with length 0 and a non-NULL data pointer (or a zero-length allocation), this dereference reads one byte out of bounds. An attacker supplying malicious EC parameters could trigger an out-of-bounds read, potentially leading to information disclosure or a crash.

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Division by zero if desttypesize parameter is zero
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function checks if typesize is zero and returns early, but does not check if the desttypesize parameter is zero before using it as a divisor in the expression 2147483647/desttypesize. If a caller passes desttypesize=0, this causes a division-by-zero, resulting in a crash (denial of service). The earlier (void) desttypesize cast misleadingly suggests the parameter is unused, yet it is subsequently used in an arithmetic check without validation.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Memory and file handle leak when ScaKwdTab fails
vulnerabilities.long_description: When ScaKwdTab(msh) returns 0 (failure), the function returns 0 without freeing the allocated `msh` structure or closing the open file handle (msh->hdl or msh->FilDes). Every other error path in the function either calls free(msh) directly or uses longjmp to reach the cleanup block, but this path bypasses both. An attacker who can supply a malformed mesh file that causes ScaKwdTab to fail could trigger repeated memory and file descriptor leaks, potentially leading to denial of service through resource exhaustion.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

[15840.675016] BUG: unable to handle kernel paging request at fffffffb

[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Missing bounds check on 'end' in the allocation loop can write beyond valid bitmap region
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the allocation phase (after the 'found' label), when the code transitions to a new page in the 'do full u32s' while(1) loop, it sets 'end = pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32' unconditionally without checking whether 'offset + PAGE_CACHE_BITS' exceeds 'size'. This differs from the scanning phase which correctly computes a bounded 'end' based on 'size'. As a result, the code may write 0xffffffff to u32s in the bitmap beyond the valid filesystem block range, corrupting bitmap metadata and marking non-existent blocks as allocated. A specially crafted or corrupted HFS+ filesystem image with particular size/offset values could trigger this, leading to out-of-bounds writes to the page cache and potential filesystem corruption or further exploitation.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in 'end' calculation when 'size' is near U32_MAX
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression '(size + 31)' can overflow if 'size' (a u32) is close to UINT32_MAX (e.g., >= 0xFFFFFFE1). Since 'size' comes from on-disk filesystem metadata, a corrupted or malicious HFS+ image could supply such a value, causing the addition to wrap around. This would result in a miscalculated 'end' pointer, potentially pointing before the expected boundary, which could lead to reading or writing out-of-bounds memory on the kmapped page.

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer overflow in multiplication of hdr.nSubrects with record size passed to ReadFromRFBServer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The expression `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` is computed as a uint32_t multiplication. Although there is a bounds check comparing nSubrects against `RFB_BUFFER_SIZE / (4 + (BPP / 8))`, depending on the size of RFB_BUFFER_SIZE and BPP, this multiplication can still overflow the uint32_t (or int) type before being passed to ReadFromRFBServer. If an overflow occurs, a much smaller value is passed as the byte count to ReadFromRFBServer, so fewer bytes are read into client->buffer. However, the subsequent loop still iterates hdr.nSubrects times, reading far past the end of the data actually placed in the buffer, causing a heap-based buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Signed/unsigned comparison between loop counter and nSubrects may cause heap buffer over-read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The loop counter `i` is declared as `int` (signed 32-bit) while `hdr.nSubrects` is `uint32_t`. If hdr.nSubrects has a value greater than INT_MAX (e.g., due to a malicious server), the signed/unsigned comparison `i < hdr.nSubrects` can behave unexpectedly — when `i` overflows to negative, it will still appear less than the unsigned nSubrects, causing the loop to iterate far beyond the buffer bounds and reading arbitrary heap memory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation that subrectangle coordinates/dimensions fall within the parent rectangle
vulnerabilities.long_description: The subrectangle's x, y, w, and h values are taken directly from the server-supplied data without checking that (x + w <= rw) and (y + h <= rh). A malicious server can supply subrectangles that extend beyond the parent rectangle boundaries. When passed to GotFillRect, this can cause out-of-bounds writes to the framebuffer, potentially leading to memory corruption or code execution.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Arbitrary file write via crafted symlink in zip archive (Zip Slip via symlink)
vulnerabilities.long_description: When extracting a symbolic link entry, the symlink target (`originalFilePath`) is read directly from the zip stream with no validation that it points within the target directory. An attacker can craft a zip with: (1) a symlink entry 'foo' pointing to '/etc' (or any arbitrary directory), followed by (2) a regular file entry 'foo/passwd'. Entry 2 passes the `isAChildOf` path traversal check (since 'targetDirectory/foo/passwd' is lexically a child), but at extraction time 'foo' is a symlink, so the write goes to '/etc/passwd'. The `hasSymbolicPart` check only runs when `followSymlinks == FollowSymlinks::no`; when symlink following is allowed (or is the default), this attack succeeds, enabling arbitrary file overwrite outside the target directory.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unchecked array index access may lead to out-of-bounds read
vulnerabilities.long_description: The function uses `getUnchecked(index)` which performs no bounds validation on the `index` parameter. If a caller passes an out-of-range index (negative or >= size), this results in an out-of-bounds memory access. Depending on the underlying container implementation this could lead to reading arbitrary memory, null pointer dereference, or undefined behavior.

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: No validation of ELF header offsets against mapped file size leads to out-of-bounds access
vulnerabilities.long_description: The code trusts values from the ELF header (e_shoff, e_phoff, e_shstrndx, sh_offset, sh_name, etc.) without validating them against st.st_size. A crafted ELF file can set these offsets to values beyond the mapped region, causing out-of-bounds reads and potentially writes (since the mapping is PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE). This applies to both the 32-bit and 64-bit code paths.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Writing truncation marker into mmap'd buffer can corrupt data or write out of bounds
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a section name is longer than 15 characters, the code writes the string "[...]" directly into the memory-mapped ELF buffer at offset 9 from the name pointer. Since 'name' points into the mmap'd region (mapped with PROT_WRITE), this modifies the mapped data. If the name pointer is near the end of the mapped region, this strcpy could write beyond the mapped memory, causing a crash or memory corruption. Additionally, this corrupts subsequent lookups of the same section name (e.g., the later strcmp calls for ".dynstr" and ".dynamic" would fail if those names were truncated).

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' when sections are not found
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared but never initialized. If the ELF file does not contain sections named ".dynstr" or ".dynamic", these variables will contain indeterminate values. They are then used as array indices into shdr[], leading to out-of-bounds memory access. A crafted ELF without these sections would trigger undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor and memory mapping leaked on error paths
vulnerabilities.long_description: When fstat fails, the file descriptor 'fd' is leaked (not closed before returning). When mmap fails, 'fd' is also leaked. At the end of the function, neither munmap nor close is called, so even on success the resources are leaked. Repeated calls could exhaust file descriptors or virtual address space.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential stack buffer overflow in 4-byte 'flag' buffer
vulnerabilities.long_description: The 'flag' buffer is only 4 bytes. It is initialized with "   " (3 chars + null = 4 bytes exactly). The functions flag2str_sh() and flag2str() write into this buffer, but if they write more than 3 characters (plus null terminator), a stack buffer overflow occurs. The buffer size provides very little margin for the flag string representation.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Integer underflow in transmit buffer bounds check can lead to heap buffer overflow
vulnerabilities.long_description: The bounds check subtracts `(p - s->tx_packet)` from `gem_get_max_buf_len(s, true)`. Since `gem_get_max_buf_len` reads from guest-controlled device registers (which can change between loop iterations), a malicious guest can: (1) set a large max buffer length, (2) let several descriptors be processed so `p` advances well into `s->tx_packet`, (3) then reduce the max buffer length register to a value smaller than `(p - s->tx_packet)`. Because `gem_get_max_buf_len` returns an unsigned value, the subtraction underflows to a very large number, making the comparison always pass. Subsequent `address_space_read` then writes past the end of `s->tx_packet`, causing a heap buffer overflow. This could allow a malicious guest to achieve code execution in the host QEMU process.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Full DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS-sized read/write of desc_first regardless of actual descriptor length
vulnerabilities.long_description: When modifying the first descriptor of a completed packet, the code reads and writes `sizeof(desc_first)` (i.e., `DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS * 4` bytes) to/from DMA memory, while all other descriptor accesses use `gem_get_desc_len(s, false)` to determine the actual descriptor size. If the actual descriptor length is smaller than `DESC_MAX_NUM_WORDS`, the write-back of `desc_first` writes uninitialized or stale stack data (the tail of the `desc_first` array beyond the real descriptor size) into guest DMA memory, and also overwrites adjacent DMA memory that may belong to the next descriptor or other data structures.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds memory access via unchecked ELF header offsets
vulnerabilities.long_description: The values e_shoff, e_phoff, e_shstrndx, e_shnum, and e_phnum are read directly from the untrusted ELF file and used as offsets/indices into the memory-mapped buffer without any validation that they fall within the mapped region (0 to st.st_size). A crafted ELF file can set these to arbitrary values, causing out-of-bounds reads and potentially crashes or information disclosure. For example, e_shoff could point beyond the file size, and e_shstrndx could index beyond the section header array.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Use of uninitialized variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' when sections are missing
vulnerabilities.long_description: The variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared but not initialized. If the ELF file does not contain sections named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic', these variables retain indeterminate values and are subsequently used as indices into the shdr array (e.g., shdr[dynamic].sh_offset). This leads to undefined behavior, out-of-bounds array access, and potential arbitrary memory read, which could cause crashes or information leaks.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Out-of-bounds write when truncating section names near the end of the mapped region
vulnerabilities.long_description: When a section name is longer than 15 characters, the code writes the string "[...]" (6 bytes including null) at offset 9 within the name. Since 'name' points into the memory-mapped file (MAP_PRIVATE with PROT_WRITE), this write succeeds but no check is performed to ensure that the write location (name + 9 through name + 14) is within the mapped region. A crafted ELF could position the string table such that 'name' starts near the end of the mapped region, causing a write past the end of the mapping and potentially a crash or memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Unvalidated name pointer used in section-to-segment mapping loop
vulnerabilities.long_description: In the 'Section to segment mapping' loop, the name pointer is computed from shstrtab.sh_offset and shdr[j].sh_name without any bounds validation (unlike the earlier section header loop which calls validated_offset). This means a crafted ELF with malicious sh_name values could cause an out-of-bounds read when printf accesses the string, potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: File descriptor leak on fstat or mmap failure
vulnerabilities.long_description: If fstat() or mmap() fails, the function returns -1 without closing the file descriptor opened by the earlier open() call. Repeated invocations with failing inputs will exhaust the process's file descriptor limit, leading to denial of service. Additionally, at the end of the function, neither close(fd) nor munmap() is called on the success path.

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.
The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Heap out-of-bounds read when colormap has zero entries
vulnerabilities.long_description: When tga_info.colormap_type is non-zero and tga_info.colormap_length is 0 with tga_info.colormap_index also 0, image->colors becomes 0. AcquireImageColormap is called with 0 colors, potentially allocating a zero-size buffer. During pixel decoding, when colormap_type != 0, the code accesses image->colormap via ConstrainColormapIndex. With 0 colors, any colormap access is an out-of-bounds heap read, potentially leaking sensitive memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Uninitialized memory exposure in image comment if ReadBlob returns fewer bytes than requested
vulnerabilities.long_description: The comment buffer is allocated with size length+MagickPathExtent, and ReadBlob is called to read tga_info.id_length bytes. However, the return value (count) is not checked to verify all bytes were read. If ReadBlob returns fewer bytes than requested (e.g., due to truncated file), the remaining bytes in the comment buffer contain uninitialized heap memory. The null terminator is placed at comment[tga_info.id_length] regardless, so the uninitialized bytes between the actual read count and id_length are included when the comment is set as an image property, potentially leaking heap contents.

vulnerabilities.short_description: Potential undefined behavior from large bit shift when bits_per_pixel equals platform word size
vulnerabilities.long_description: When colormap_type is 0 and image is PseudoClass, image->colors is computed as (size_t)1 << tga_info.bits_per_pixel. The validation allows bits_per_pixel values of 24 and 32. On a 32-bit platform where size_t is 32 bits, shifting by 32 is undefined behavior per the C standard (shift count >= width of type). Even on 64-bit platforms, shifting by 32 produces 4294967296 colormap entries which could lead to excessive memory allocation (denial of service). The existing overflow check against ~0UL/sizeof(*image->colormap) may not adequately prevent this on 64-bit systems.
